roots.title: Course Surveys and Introduction to Clustering Techniques
roots.summary: Students are reminded to complete the campus final course survey and the internal core survey for a potential 1% bonus towards the final grade, with feedback encouraged for course improvement. The introduction to clustering as an unsupervised learning technique is presented, highlighting its distinction from supervised learning, which involves mapping input features to output targets. Clustering identifies patterns in unlabeled data and will cover two main algorithms, their loss functions, and applications, along with unsupervised learning techniques like dimensionality reduction, particularly principal component analysis (PCA). The importance of clustering algorithms in image compression is discussed, showcasing how they simplify large digital images by grouping pixels based on RGB values, improving color accuracy as the number of clusters increases. Additionally, the necessity of compression for digital images is emphasized, with clustering techniques reducing unique colors while maintaining visual quality. Other methods such as transform-based, prediction-based, and deep learning-based approaches are also mentioned, along with the application of clustering in social networks to categorize individuals based on interactions.

children.title: Course Surveys and Introduction to Clustering
children.summary: Students are reminded to complete the campus final course survey and the internal core survey, accessible via a QR code or link, to earn a 0.5% bonus towards the final grade, with an additional 0.5% for 90% class participation. Feedback is encouraged for course improvement, with surveys due by the end of RR week on Sunday. The introduction to clustering as an unsupervised learning technique distinguishes it from supervised learning, which maps input features to output targets. Clustering seeks to identify patterns in data without labeled outputs. The session will address two main clustering algorithms, their loss functions, and applications, along with unsupervised learning techniques such as dimensionality reduction, particularly principal component analysis (PCA), emphasizing the lack of input/output pairs in unsupervised learning.

children.title: Course Surveys and Introduction to Clustering
children.summary: Students are reminded to complete the campus final course survey and the internal core survey, which can be accessed via a QR code or link. Completing both surveys will earn a 0.5% bonus towards the final grade, with an additional 0.5% for achieving 90% class participation. Feedback is encouraged for course improvement, and the surveys are due by the end of RR week on Sunday. The lecture introduces clustering as a key unsupervised learning technique, differentiating it from supervised learning, which maps input features to output targets. Clustering aims to find patterns in data without labeled outputs. The session will cover two main clustering algorithms, their loss functions, and applications, as well as unsupervised learning techniques like dimensionality reduction, particularly principal component analysis (PCA), highlighting the absence of input/output pairs in unsupervised learning.

children.title: Final Course Survey and Bonus Points Announcement
children.summary: Students are reminded to complete both the campus final course survey and the internal core survey, accessible via a QR code or link. Completing both surveys earns a 0.5% bonus point towards the final grade, with an additional 0.5% bonus for 90% class participation. Feedback is encouraged for course improvement, and surveys are due by the end of RR week on Sunday.

children.title: Final Course Survey and Bonus Points Announcement
children.summary: Students are reminded to complete the campus final course survey and the internal core survey. Scanning the provided QR code or accessing the link will allow them to participate. Completing both surveys grants each student a 0.5% bonus point towards their final grade. If 90% of the class participates, an additional 0.5% bonus will be awarded to those who completed both surveys. Feedback is encouraged to improve the course for future semesters, and the surveys are due by the end of the RR week on Sunday.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Good morning everyone. Thanks for coming to our very last lecture before Thanksgiving. So today's topic is very interesting. So as we promise every week is going to have an entirely new topic after the term and today is just going to be a whole new topic. But before I get into the topic for today, which is clustering, I want to remind you of the campus final course survey and also our internal core survey. So if you scan this QR code or if you go to that link and there's also a post on Ed that you can get access to the internal. Anonymous course e-vow that you can fill out. If you fill out those internal survey and also the campus survey, then each individual person is going to get 0.5% extra bonus point towards your final course grade. Because the class is not curved, that's a really good deal. The other deal that we usually offer in Data 100 is that if 90% of the class fills out those of the surveys, both internal and the campus survey then we're going to offer everyone who fill that both at a survey and additional 0.5%. So that's a whole percentage point that can... put you in a different great then. We're really interested in getting feedback from different elements of the course that you like or the things that you are suggesting that the improve. In the future semesters again because I keep teaching the course every semester that I can actually get that feedback and implemented and then keep improving the course. I feel like data 100 is now at the stable point where like the homeworks and the core column and everything is a stable but again. any feedback that you have in terms of refining the course a little bit further that will help us greatly. So the link is there. There's all same permission on Ed. So please fill out both of the surveys. I think both of them or the campus surveys going to be due on Sunday. At the end of the RR week so that's the deadline that you usually have for being able to complete both of the service. Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: Good morning everyone. Thanks for coming to our very last lecture before Thanksgiving. So today's topic is very interesting. So as we promise every week is going to have an entirely new topic after the term and today is just going to be a whole new topic. But before I get into the topic for today, which is clustering, I want to remind you of the campus final course survey and also our internal core survey. So if you scan this QR code or if you go to that link and there's also a post on Ed that you can get access to the internal. Anonymous course e-vow that you can fill out. If you fill out those internal survey and also the campus survey, then each individual person is going to get 0.5% extra bonus point towards your final course grade. Because the class is not curved, that's a really good deal. The other deal that we usually offer in Data 100 is that if 90% of the class fills out those of the surveys, both internal and the campus survey then we're going to offer everyone who fill that both at a survey and additional 0.5%. So that's a whole percentage point that can... put you in a different great then. We're really interested in getting feedback from different elements of the course that you like or the things that you are suggesting that the improve. In the future semesters again because I keep teaching the course every semester that I can actually get that feedback and implemented and then keep improving the course. I feel like data 100 is now at the stable point where like the homeworks and the core column and everything is a stable but again. any feedback that you have in terms of refining the course a little bit further that will help us greatly. So the link is there. There's all same permission on Ed. So please fill out both of the surveys. I think both of them or the campus surveys going to be due on Sunday. At the end of the RR week so that's the deadline that you usually have for being able to complete both of the service.

children.title: Introduction to Clustering and Unsupervised Learning
children.summary: The lecture introduces clustering as a key unsupervised learning technique, highlighting its importance in advanced modeling and data pre-processing. It differentiates between supervised learning, which involves mapping input features to output targets in tasks like regression and classification, and unsupervised learning, which seeks to find patterns in data without labeled outputs. The session will discuss two main clustering algorithms and their respective loss functions, alongside applications of clustering. Additionally, it covers unsupervised learning techniques such as dimensionality reduction, specifically principal component analysis (PCA), and emphasizes the lack of input/output pairs in unsupervised learning compared to supervised learning.

children.title: Introduction to Clustering and Unsupervised Learning
children.summary: The lecture introduces clustering as a powerful unsupervised learning technique, emphasizing its role in sophisticated modeling and data pre-processing. It outlines the distinction between supervised and unsupervised learning, explaining that supervised learning involves algorithms that map input features to output targets, such as in regression and classification tasks. The session will cover two main clustering algorithms and their loss functions, with applications in various tasks.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So with that added away today's lecture 26 so we're going to talk about clustering so this is just one lecture on one topic. I'll try to cover a few things or two main algorithms and treat loss functions and clustering which is very exciting. and then we will move on to neural networks, onto user lecture and then we'll come back on the Thursday lecture of the last week of the instruction to talk about large language models and also wrap up data 100 for you. So I'm going to start by just introducing clustering and giving you some examples of different tasks that we can do with clustering. Clustering is a really powerful technique in terms of the fact that it can do sophisticated modeling for us, and it can also help us do pre-processing in the data. And it has also great categories of applications as well. So in terms of where we are in the entire taxonomy of machine learning, we had seen a bunch of supervised learning algorithms, and we had also seen one example of unsupervised learning algorithm. So what you're seeing in the right hand side is that clustering is also an unsupervised learning algorithm. So what was supervised learning algorithm? Remember that supervised learning algorithm is any algorithm that is learning a function that maps an input to an output. So for example, you have input on different house features and you're doing house price prediction. So that's the regression task. We have also seen classification tasks using logistic regression, right? So all of those cases that we have input features and then we also have targets in terms of outputs that can be real value numbers or scalar values or classification, those are categories of supervised learning algorithms. leaf_chunk.audio: So with that added away today's lecture 26 so we're going to talk about clustering so this is just one lecture on one topic. I'll try to cover a few things or two main algorithms and treat loss functions and clustering which is very exciting. and then we will move on to neural networks, onto user lecture and then we'll come back on the Thursday lecture of the last week of the instruction to talk about large language models and also wrap up data 100 for you. So I'm going to start by just introducing clustering and giving you some examples of different tasks that we can do with clustering. Clustering is a really powerful technique in terms of the fact that it can do sophisticated modeling for us, and it can also help us do pre-processing in the data. And it has also great categories of applications as well. So in terms of where we are in the entire taxonomy of machine learning, we had seen a bunch of supervised learning algorithms, and we had also seen one example of unsupervised learning algorithm. So what you're seeing in the right hand side is that clustering is also an unsupervised learning algorithm. So what was supervised learning algorithm? Remember that supervised learning algorithm is any algorithm that is learning a function that maps an input to an output. So for example, you have input on different house features and you're doing house price prediction. So that's the regression task. We have also seen classification tasks using logistic regression, right? So all of those cases that we have input features and then we also have targets in terms of outputs that can be real value numbers or scalar values or classification, those are categories of supervised learning algorithms.

children.title: Overview of Unsupervised Learning Techniques
children.summary: Unsupervised learning algorithms focus on identifying patterns in data without labeled outputs. Key techniques include dimensionality reduction, such as principal component analysis (PCA), which simplifies the feature space for modeling and visualization. Clustering is another important method discussed, where data points are segmented into distinct categories or clusters. The lecture also contrasts unsupervised learning with supervised learning, emphasizing the absence of input/output pairs in the former.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: In unsupervised learning algorithms, we talked about the fact that either we don't have the labels or we don't want to use the labels, but we're just using the characteristics and the data in order to learn some information from the underlying pattern in our feature space. We had seen dimensional reproduction using principal component analysis in the previous week, which is very helpful in terms of reducing dimensionality of your feature space into principal components that then you can use in order to do modeling before you go into any modeling or before you do any kind of visualization in the data. We also have clustering that we're going to talk about today, which in that case, we are trying to segment our data points into different clusters or categories. children.title: Clustering Algorithms in Image Compression
children.summary: Clustering algorithms identify patterns in unlabeled data and are essential for image compression by simplifying large digital images. They reduce color features by grouping pixels based on RGB values into clusters, allowing similar colors to be represented by a single color. An example with a cat image demonstrates how starting with two color clusters can evolve into more detailed representations, improving color accuracy as the number of clusters increases.

children.title: Clustering Algorithms in Image Compression
children.summary: Clustering algorithms identify patterns in unlabeled data and are particularly useful in image compression by simplifying large digital images that require significant storage. This process reduces color features by categorizing pixels based on RGB values into clusters, grouping similar colors to be represented by a single color. An example using a cat image illustrates how starting with two color clusters can evolve into more detailed representations, enhancing color accuracy with an increasing number of clusters.

children.title: Clustering Algorithms in Image Compression
children.summary: Clustering algorithms identify patterns in unlabeled data, particularly useful in image compression. Digital images are large and require significant storage, so clustering simplifies visual information by reducing color features. This process involves categorizing pixels based on RGB values into clusters, where similar colors are grouped and represented by a single color. An example with a cat image demonstrates how starting with two color clusters can evolve into more detailed representations, enhancing color accuracy as the number of clusters increases.

children.title: Clustering Algorithms and Image Compression
children.summary: Clustering algorithms are used to identify patterns in unlabeled data, often discarding any available labels during the process. Three examples of clustering applications were introduced, with a focus on image compression. Digital images are typically large, requiring significant storage and bandwidth for transmission and storage. Clustering can simplify visual information by reducing the number of color features or channels, allowing images to be compressed while still conveying essential content.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: or categories. So we're going to take a look at different kinds of examples of clustering algorithms that will help us do that. But again, in clustering algorithms, we're assuming that we don't have the labels either. We don't have the labels or we're just discarding the label and not using them in the clustering kind of process. So let me walk you through three examples. One example is image compression. So digital images are generally large in order to transmit them over the web or store them. We're spending a lot of resources. children.title: Image Compression Using Color Clustering
children.summary: Image compression can be achieved through clustering, which simplifies color information by categorizing pixels based on their RGB values. Pixels with similar colors are grouped into clusters, and each cluster is represented by a single unified color, effectively reducing color diversity. An example using an image of a cat illustrates this process, showing how starting with two color clusters can evolve into more detailed representations as the number of clusters increases, enhancing color accuracy in the compressed image.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: One way to do it is using clustering. This is just one way to do doing image compression or different techniques that I'm going to talk about them briefly next. But one way to do it is to simplify color information that we have in the images. So what if we can use the pixels on the image? So each pixel has a color, right? RGB value, if it's a color, a colored image. And what we can do is actually cluster or categorize pixels with different kind of color values into different clusters and then simplify the color in each cluster using only a unified or a single color. So meaning that we're grouping similar colors together and then we're going to represent all of the colors in one cluster using the color in the center of that cluster. That's just a representation. So the diversity of the colors that you might see like different versions of red or different versions of black or gray are going to be simplified into like one kind of representative color for that specific cluster. If the lecture has to have something about my cat, so this is an example of image compression that I ran on the picture of Pishi. And what you can see is the original image. So I always like to think about Pishi as being only having two colors. But as you're seeing the compressed image on the right hand side, depending on the number of clusters, it starts from two and then it goes all over until, I'm not sure if you're seeing that, oh, if you take a look at, for example, the... the color of the eye. It starts from two clusters to be like grayish, whiteish, and then it goes all the way to actually showing the green kind of color. So that's kind of how we're simplifying colors. So all of these pixels are clustered together into two clusters initially. So probably black and white or like a representative of black, brown, the entire pixel under a representative white, and then it tries to diversify as I increase the resolution or as I increase the number of clusters that it can accept. leaf_chunk.audio: One way to do it is using clustering. This is just one way to do doing image compression or different techniques that I'm going to talk about them briefly next. But one way to do it is to simplify color information that we have in the images. So what if we can use the pixels on the image? So each pixel has a color, right? RGB value, if it's a color, a colored image. And what we can do is actually cluster or categorize pixels with different kind of color values into different clusters and then simplify the color in each cluster using only a unified or a single color. So meaning that we're grouping similar colors together and then we're going to represent all of the colors in one cluster using the color in the center of that cluster. That's just a representation. So the diversity of the colors that you might see like different versions of red or different versions of black or gray are going to be simplified into like one kind of representative color for that specific cluster. If the lecture has to have something about my cat, so this is an example of image compression that I ran on the picture of Pishi. And what you can see is the original image. So I always like to think about Pishi as being only having two colors. But as you're seeing the compressed image on the right hand side, depending on the number of clusters, it starts from two and then it goes all over until, I'm not sure if you're seeing that, oh, if you take a look at, for example, the... the color of the eye. It starts from two clusters to be like grayish, whiteish, and then it goes all the way to actually showing the green kind of color. So that's kind of how we're simplifying colors. So all of these pixels are clustered together into two clusters initially. So probably black and white or like a representative of black, brown, the entire pixel under a representative white, and then it tries to diversify as I increase the resolution or as I increase the number of clusters that it can accept.

children.title: Image Compression and Clustering Techniques Overview
children.summary: Digital images require significant storage, making compression essential. Techniques include clustering to reduce unique colors by grouping similar ones, represented by centroids, while maintaining visual quality. Other methods discussed are transform-based, prediction-based, and deep learning-based approaches. In social networks, clustering categorizes individuals based on interactions to identify communities, aiding in connection recommendations and user analysis, illustrated by the 'karate club' network example. Clustering techniques are also applied in climate data analysis to identify trends, with an example of grouping temperature data into four clusters. Clustering serves as both a modeling technique and a preprocessing step for feature extraction. Algorithms are categorized into hierarchical and partitional types, with hierarchical clustering merging data points and partitional methods like k-means clustering partitioning data into distinct clusters. K-Means involves choosing a number of clusters, initializing centers, and iteratively adjusting them based on proximity until convergence. A distinction is made between K-Means, an unsupervised learning method for clustering, and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), a classification method.

K-Means involves choosing a number of clusters, initializing centers, and iteratively adjusting them based on proximity until convergence. A distinction is made between K-Means, an unsupervised learning method for clustering, and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), a classification method.

children.title: Image Compression and Social Network Clustering
children.summary: Digital images require significant storage, making compression essential. Techniques include clustering to reduce unique colors by grouping similar ones, represented by centroids, while maintaining visual quality. Other methods discussed are transform-based, prediction-based, and deep learning-based approaches. In social networks, clustering categorizes individuals based on interactions to identify communities, aiding in connection recommendations and user analysis. The 'karate club' network example illustrates how clustering reveals interaction patterns and community structures.

children.title: Image Compression Techniques Overview
children.summary: Digital images require significant storage and bandwidth, making image compression essential. Clustering techniques can reduce the number of unique colors in an image by grouping similar colors into clusters, where each cluster is represented by a color centroid. This process simplifies images while maintaining their visual appeal. Other image compression methods mentioned include transform-based, prediction-based, and deep learning-based approaches.

children.title: Clustering in Social Networks and Its Applications
children.summary: Clustering in social networks involves categorizing individuals based on their interactions to identify groups or communities. This technique is useful for community modeling, making connection recommendations, and conducting user analysis without prior labels. An example discussed is the 'karate club' network, where members are represented as nodes and their interactions as edges. By clustering these individuals, one can discern patterns of interaction and learn about community structures.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Clustering can also be used in social networks. So if you have a social network, and a social network platform, and you want to basically categorize individuals who have more interactions with each other into different clusters, and do some kind of community modeling, or be able to make recommendations of like, this is a connection recommendation for a person, because like you interact with these individuals, and this individual, this third individual is also kind of interacting with the same group of people, maybe you want to connect with them and make those kinds of recommendations or also doing some marketing to a cluster with the specific characteristics, kind of user analysis. All of these kinds of studies require doing some sort of clustering because we don't have the labels. We don't actually know for fact whether individuals are within a cluster or a community or not. One thing that we We can look at the interactions that they have with each other and then be able to cluster behaviors or cluster communities separately. This is an example like synthetic example from, for example, something that's called karate club. What you can see is that the notes on this kind of network are members of the club and then the edges are interactions that they have with them. And if you try to actually cluster all of these individuals into four different clusters, what you would see is that, okay, these are the people that these individuals are going to have more interaction with. And then if you want to learn communities and information from it, this is one way that you can do so. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

leaf_chunk.audio: Clustering can also be used in social networks. So if you have a social network, and a social network platform, and you want to basically categorize individuals who have more interactions with each other into different clusters, and do some kind of community modeling, or be able to make recommendations of like, this is a connection recommendation for a person, because like you interact with these individuals, and this individual, this third individual is also kind of interacting with the same group of people, maybe you want to connect with them and make those kinds of recommendations or also doing some marketing to a cluster with the specific characteristics, kind of user analysis. All of these kinds of studies require doing some sort of clustering because we don't have the labels. We don't actually know for fact whether individuals are within a cluster or a community or not. One thing that we We can look at the interactions that they have with each other and then be able to cluster behaviors or cluster communities separately. This is an example like synthetic example from, for example, something that's called karate club. What you can see is that the notes on this kind of network are members of the club and then the edges are interactions that they have with them. And if you try to actually cluster all of these individuals into four different clusters, what you would see is that, okay, these are the people that these individuals are going to have more interaction with. And then if you want to learn communities and information from it, this is one way that you can do so.

children.title: Clustering Applications and Algorithms Overview
children.summary: Clustering techniques are applied in climate data analysis to identify trends and support environmental modeling, exemplified by grouping temperature data into four clusters to reveal similar patterns. Clustering serves as both a modeling technique and a preprocessing step for feature extraction. Clustering algorithms are categorized into hierarchical and partitional types, with hierarchical clustering merging data points into larger clusters, while partitional methods, like k-means clustering, partition data into distinct clusters. K-means involves choosing a number of clusters, initializing centers, and iteratively adjusting them based on data point proximity until convergence. Visual demonstrations and demo code will be available on the course website.

children.title: Clustering Applications in Climate Data Analysis
children.summary: Plastering can be applied to large data sets, such as climate data, to identify regional and global climate trends and support environmental modeling. An example involves clustering temperature data from various regions into four groups, revealing similar temperature patterns among them. This method efficiently analyzes large unlabeled data sets, providing valuable insights. Clustering can serve as both a modeling technique and a preprocessing step for feature extraction, where cluster assignments can be utilized in subsequent modeling tasks. Although this concept extends beyond the scope of Data 100, it highlights the interconnectedness of the topics learned in the course.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: One last example is that plastering can also be used on large data sets such as climate data sets and be used in climate sciences. So if you want to for example identify regional climate trends or global climate trends and be able to do environmental modeling and things like that and do monitoring of the environmental trends, we can actually do that with clustering. So this is an example that I ran on a data set that has the temperature data or average temperature data from different regions. And if you go ahead and cluster them into four different groups and four different groups are also shown by that color map from zero the way to tree. What you're seeing is that, okay, these different regions now show a similar pattern in terms of the temperature. so you can kind of find and validate, okay, like, whether the point on California has the same temperature and has similar temperature kind of patterns. Compared to Hawaii or not so this is kind of one example that a simple algorithm can handle a very large unlabeled data set very efficiently to provide some kind of information for us to use. I also want to put it out there that clustering Hand by itself be the modeling that you are doing on a data set or it can be used as a pre-processing that you will do in order to do feature extraction So you could do clustering, learn clusters, and use the cluster assignment as a feature of the data. And then later on, you can actually use that in the downstream kind of any modeling that you want to do. This is kind of beyond the scope for data 100, but just know that everything that you're learning can be pieces of puzzle that you're putting together in order to do proper modeling and proper feature engineering altogether. leaf_chunk.audio: One last example is that plastering can also be used on large data sets such as climate data sets and be used in climate sciences. So if you want to for example identify regional climate trends or global climate trends and be able to do environmental modeling and things like that and do monitoring of the environmental trends, we can actually do that with clustering. So this is an example that I ran on a data set that has the temperature data or average temperature data from different regions. And if you go ahead and cluster them into four different groups and four different groups are also shown by that color map from zero the way to tree. What you're seeing is that, okay, these different regions now show a similar pattern in terms of the temperature. so you can kind of find and validate, okay, like, whether the point on California has the same temperature and has similar temperature kind of patterns. Compared to Hawaii or not so this is kind of one example that a simple algorithm can handle a very large unlabeled data set very efficiently to provide some kind of information for us to use. I also want to put it out there that clustering Hand by itself be the modeling that you are doing on a data set or it can be used as a pre-processing that you will do in order to do feature extraction So you could do clustering, learn clusters, and use the cluster assignment as a feature of the data. And then later on, you can actually use that in the downstream kind of any modeling that you want to do. This is kind of beyond the scope for data 100, but just know that everything that you're learning can be pieces of puzzle that you're putting together in order to do proper modeling and proper feature engineering altogether.

children.title: Taxonomy of Clustering Algorithms
children.summary: Clustering algorithms can be categorized into two main types: hierarchical and partitional. Hierarchical clustering starts with each data point as its own cluster and merges them progressively to form larger clusters until the desired number of clusters is reached. This approach allows for a flexible organization of data points based on their similarities.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So let's now get into talking about different versions of clustering algorithms that we'll see and what is kind of the general taxonomy of clustering approaches. In clustering, what we have is we have two separate kind of categories of learning. There is one category of learning that is hierarchical and there is another category of learning algorithms that are partitional. So hierarchical means that what we're trying to do is we're trying to assume that all of the data points that we have, all of those unlabeled data points, each individual data point is one cluster. So initially, if you're starting from a training set of size n, for example, you have n different clusters. If your goal is to go down to having only four clusters or three clusters, what we will do is hierarchically, we'll go ahead and link data points together and combine them together and will form more complex and more complex or bigger and bigger clusters, a plentil the point that we have four or three or however many clusters that you want. children.title: Partitional Approaches to Clustering
children.summary: Partitional approaches in clustering aim to partition a large dataset into distinct clusters. This contrasts with hierarchical approaches, which can be either bottom-up or top-down. The focus will be on k-means clustering, which is a simple and effective algorithm. Visuals will be provided to demonstrate how the algorithm operates, and additional demo code will be available on the course website.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We're going to talk about different kind of linking mechanisms and different ways that we'll try to combine clusters together in the second half of today's lecture. In terms of the Partitional Approaches, Partitional Approaches generally assume that you have a big data set and you want to find a criteria to partition different parts into different clusters. It's a different approach. So there is bottom-up approach versus top-to-bottom approach that we have between hierarchical and partitional approaches. There are different versions of partitional approaches and for the purpose of data 100 we're going to only see one algorithm of that category and we're going to talk about k-minis clustering. It's going to be very useful, it's going to be very simple. I'm going to walk you through the algorithm and I have visuals to show you how the algorithm actually runs In the demo code that I will not go over in the lecture, but you can actually see it on the website. children.title: K-Means Clustering Overview and Implementation
children.summary: K-means clustering is introduced as a widely used clustering approach where an arbitrary number of clusters, k, is chosen, and k centers are randomly placed. The algorithm iterates by coloring data points based on the nearest center and then adjusting the centers to the mean of the colored points until convergence is achieved. The segment also mentions the availability of off-the-shelf K-means functions for practical application.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You can actually see how it can be implemented. But then again, there is off-the-shelf K-means function that you can use in order to apply K-means to a data set and then transform your data into assigning it into different clusters. children.title: K-means Clustering Initialization and Steps
children.summary: K-means clustering involves an initialization phase and a two-step training process. The initialization phase requires selecting a hyperparameter, K, which represents the number of clusters. Initially, K centers are randomly placed in the feature space. This random placement is akin to starting from an arbitrary point and incrementally adjusting to find optimal centers. The algorithm then proceeds with two main steps: first, cluster assignment, where data points are allocated to the nearest cluster center to segment the dataset effectively. The goal is to ensure that points are closer to their respective centers.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: K-min's clustering has three pieces. There is initialization piece and there is two step in the training piece. So the initialization piece says that assume that you want to cluster your data set into K different clusters, right? So this is a hyperparameter. By now you know what hyperparameters mean. This is something that you're deciding ahead of time, and we're going to talk about how we can actually figure out and try to optimize for the right value for k. But let's assume that we have a k that we picked as a priori. And then what we're going to do is we will say that I'm going to randomly place k centers on the feature space. So this is the initialization phase. This is kind of similar to us doing great in this then we had no idea where we're going but we tried to actually do that. Uh do initialization from some point for all the parameters and then we were incrementally trying to update and do a local search version in order to get to the optimum. This is kind of the same thing we don't know how to get to the optimum but what we can do is that we we can say that I'm going to actually start from a random place and then incrementally move these centers to the right places that they should be. So that's why the initialization is random. So we randomly place K centers and then the actual algorithm starts. The actual algorithm has two parts. There is a part that it says that once you have the centers on the future space, let's go ahead and uh, color all the points according to the closest center. This is that is called cluster assignment. So I have all of those clusters. I put all of those cluster centers and now I just want to associate points to the centers. It's kind of natural that I'm going to allocate points to the cluster that is closest to that point because I want to actually have a sense of segmenting this entire data set and then segmenting means that the data points should be closer to their centers. So that's the first thing. The second step that I would do is after all of the points are assigned to those clusters, what I would do is I would double check to make sure that the centers are actually the centers of those clusters. leaf_chunk.audio: K-min's clustering has three pieces. There is initialization piece and there is two step in the training piece. So the initialization piece says that assume that you want to cluster your data set into K different clusters, right? So this is a hyperparameter. By now you know what hyperparameters mean. This is something that you're deciding ahead of time, and we're going to talk about how we can actually figure out and try to optimize for the right value for k. But let's assume that we have a k that we picked as a priori. And then what we're going to do is we will say that I'm going to randomly place k centers on the feature space. So this is the initialization phase. This is kind of similar to us doing great in this then we had no idea where we're going but we tried to actually do that. Uh do initialization from some point for all the parameters and then we were incrementally trying to update and do a local search version in order to get to the optimum. This is kind of the same thing we don't know how to get to the optimum but what we can do is that we we can say that I'm going to actually start from a random place and then incrementally move these centers to the right places that they should be. So that's why the initialization is random. So we randomly place K centers and then the actual algorithm starts. The actual algorithm has two parts. There is a part that it says that once you have the centers on the future space, let's go ahead and uh, color all the points according to the closest center. This is that is called cluster assignment. So I have all of those clusters. I put all of those cluster centers and now I just want to associate points to the centers. It's kind of natural that I'm going to allocate points to the cluster that is closest to that point because I want to actually have a sense of segmenting this entire data set and then segmenting means that the data points should be closer to their centers. So that's the first thing. The second step that I would do is after all of the points are assigned to those clusters, what I would do is I would double check to make sure that the centers are actually the centers of those clusters.

children.title: K-Means Clustering Initialization and Assignment
children.summary: The K-Means clustering algorithm involves initializing k random centers in a feature space, which can be anywhere in relation to the data points. An example is provided using a two-dimensional dataset for visualization simplicity. After initialization, points are assigned to the nearest center based on distance, effectively coloring them according to their closest cluster center. The process is repeated until convergence, ensuring that points are consistently assigned to the appropriate clusters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Because we can randomly initialize centers somewhere that is not necessarily the center of the data points, but once we allocate points into the clusters, we might want to move the centers back to the center of the points that are assigned to those clusters. That's a lot of words. Let's see an example. So the example is that this is my raw data set. As you can see, there is no label nothing going on here. And this is a two-dimensional feature space. We can run clustering in a higher dimensional space and it can actually handle it. But for the visualization purpose, I picked this data set that only the two features and this data says that the visualization is simpler. So the initialization step says that go ahead and initialize two random centers. We'll do that. And as you can imagine, because we did not control for the initialization piece, the centers can actually be initialized anywhere. But the thing that we'll do in the next step is that we're going to actually go ahead and color the points according to the closest center of the cluster. This is just... Class of assignment. So what I'll do is I'll basically go ahead and say that okay looking at this point right here I'm looking at the distance of this point to the center blue and the center orange and it looks like that the center blue is closer so I'm going to color it as blue But for example, I point right here is closer to the orange Center so it's going to be colored as orange So what that's what we're going to do and this this is like the Uh assignments that these points are going to get. leaf_chunk.audio: Because we can randomly initialize centers somewhere that is not necessarily the center of the data points, but once we allocate points into the clusters, we might want to move the centers back to the center of the points that are assigned to those clusters. That's a lot of words. Let's see an example. So the example is that this is my raw data set. As you can see, there is no label nothing going on here. And this is a two-dimensional feature space. We can run clustering in a higher dimensional space and it can actually handle it. But for the visualization purpose, I picked this data set that only the two features and this data says that the visualization is simpler. So the initialization step says that go ahead and initialize two random centers. We'll do that. And as you can imagine, because we did not control for the initialization piece, the centers can actually be initialized anywhere. But the thing that we'll do in the next step is that we're going to actually go ahead and color the points according to the closest center of the cluster. This is just... Class of assignment. So what I'll do is I'll basically go ahead and say that okay looking at this point right here I'm looking at the distance of this point to the center blue and the center orange and it looks like that the center blue is closer so I'm going to color it as blue But for example, I point right here is closer to the orange Center so it's going to be colored as orange So what that's what we're going to do and this this is like the Uh assignments that these points are going to get.

children.title: K-Means Clustering Algorithm Steps
children.summary: The K-Means clustering algorithm involves an iterative process where an arbitrary number of centers (k) are randomly initialized. In each iteration, data points are assigned to the nearest center based on distance, and then the centers are updated to the mean of the assigned points. This process of cluster assignment and center update is repeated until convergence is achieved, meaning the centers no longer change significantly. The discussion includes the importance of moving the centers to accurately reflect the clusters formed by the data points.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Now you can see why I have the second step of moving the centers to the center of the clusters, right? Because now my centers are not necessarily center centers, so they need to be moved to be the center of those points. So what we'll do is we'll take a look at this cluster right here. We will take a look at this cluster right here. We will calculate the center actually, and then we'll move these data points to the center of those clusters that they have. This is one full iteration. Eteration 0 is just randomly initialized in case centers, in iteration 1 what we did was center cluster assignment center update. Then we'll do again cluster assignment center update. And we'll repeat this process until convergence. And we're going to talk about what convergence is going to look like. So repeating these two steps that we have, up until the point that we see convergence. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

children.title: K-Means Clustering Algorithm Explanation
children.summary: Convergence in K-Means clustering occurs when the centers and colors remain unchanged, indicating that further iterations will not alter the results. The algorithm involves randomly placing k centers and iterating until convergence by coloring points based on proximity to the nearest center and adjusting the centers to the mean of their assigned points. During the first iteration, the positions of the centers change, and tracking the blue cluster's center is emphasized for clarity in observing differences.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Convergence generally means that if the centers don't move or the colors don't move, that's the point that we would say that we have reached convergence. And after that point, if you run it further, then nothing is going to change, so you might as well want to end that. I'll go right them right there. Okay, let's go down for more steps. So that was iteration one. Now my centers are in a different position. So if I go ahead and color the points, then the points are going to be colored like this. In order to see the differences better, I suggest that you only take a like or keep track of the center of the blue cluster and also all of these points right over here so that you can see the differences. Other points, other orange points are really hard to track. So we colored everything, but now if you take a look, for example, again, take a look at the center of the blue cluster. The issue is that the center of the blue cluster is not so much in the center because I have a blob of all of those blue points down there. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin

children.title: K-Means Clustering Algorithm Process
children.summary: The K-Means clustering algorithm involves selecting an arbitrary number of centers (k) and randomly positioning them. The process is repeated until convergence, which includes coloring data points based on their closest center and then moving each center to the average position of the points assigned to it. In this segment, the instructor demonstrates the iterative process of adjusting the centers and coloring the points, noting how the centers shift closer to the actual data distribution with each iteration. If no changes occur in the coloring after moving the centers, it indicates that convergence has been reached.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the center needs to be moved down a little bit closer to kind of aided average of the number of points that I have in the two sides of the center. So we're recolored them already and now removing them. So notice from here to here that the blue center actually shifted a little bit down. So, centers move, therefore we're expecting that now the colors need to also be changed. So what I'm going to do is color them one more time. Again, notice that now I only have only these two blue points right here, as opposed to I had like five before probably. Okay, so coloring them again and then taking a look at the centers. So the center of the blue cluster is right here, but it can actually be moved a little bit closer to be the actual center. So if I go ahead and move the centers, that's what you're going to see. So one more time, the center was the center was here and now move the little bit down. I'll go ahead again and color the clusters so coloring the clusters now is going to give me only one blue point right there And then I can actually go ahead and Move the centers one more time and if I move the centers one more time and then colored them again I won't see any changes in the coloring if I don't see changes in the coloring What does that mean with the centers move again? children.title: K-Means Clustering Convergence Explanation
children.summary: The K-Means clustering algorithm demonstrates convergence when there are no changes in color assignments or center positions. After iterations 4 and 5, although the centers moved slightly, the absence of color changes indicates that further iterations would not yield any new results, confirming that the algorithm has stabilized.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: If colors are not changing, centers are not changing. If centers are not changing, again, the colors are not going to change. So the algorithm is a stable at this point. So this is the point that the algorithm is going to converge and because there is no color change and no center change, if we went ahead and tried iteration 6 or 7 and whatnot, we would not be able to see any changes. So that shows us that there is convergence going on. and actually for iteration 4 to iteration 5. Um, we yeah, that's the same thing. Centres moved us lightly, but because the colors didn't change, um, then the centers are not going to change after iteration five, um, and moving forward. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

children.title: K-Means Clustering vs. K-Nearest Neighbors
children.summary: K-Means clustering is introduced as a powerful algorithm for handling unlabeled and noisy data, emphasizing the need to explore various clustering techniques for different datasets. A clear distinction is made between K-Means and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), highlighting that K-Means is an unsupervised learning method focused on clustering data points into K groups, while KNN is primarily a classification method that predicts the label of a data point based on the most common class among its nearest neighbors.

children.title: Overview of K-Means and Hierarchical Clustering
children.summary: K-Means clustering is introduced as the easiest algorithm in the course, noted for its power in handling unlabeled data and noisy labels. The importance of exploring combinations of clustering techniques for different data sets is emphasized. A distinction is made between K-Means and K-Nearest Neighbors, clarifying that K-Means is used for clustering by assigning points to K clusters, while K-Nearest Neighbors is primarily a classification method based on the most common class among nearby data points.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that was Cayman's clustering. That's the easiest algorithm you will learn in data 100. And actually the next one is also easy, which is the hierarchical clustering. But that's kind of the entire gist of Cayman's clustering. Just note that it's a very powerful technique. It can be applied on to unlabeled data. It has different implications for larger data sets, not unlabeled data sets, or data sets that labels are noisy and you don't want to rely on the and labels. In all of those cases, you might want to actually try a combination of different things, including doing clustering. For those who have heard about canyons neighbors, if you've not heard about it, forget about it, don't listen to me for the next 45 seconds. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterini

children.title: K-Means vs. K-Nearest Neighbors Comparison
children.summary: K-Means clustering and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) are distinct algorithms despite their similar names. K-Means is an unsupervised learning method used for clustering, where data points are assigned to one of K clusters. In contrast, KNN is primarily a classification technique that predicts the label of a data point based on the most common class among its k-nearest neighbors, and it can also be used for regression. It is emphasized that these two algorithms should not be confused.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But if you've heard about canyons in the ears neighbors in data 8, know that it's an entirely different algorithm than canyons clustering. They both have canyons, but they are entirely different things. Cameous clustering is unsupervised. It is used for clustering purposes. K-nearest neighbors is actually used mostly for classifications. Sometimes it's also used for regression as well, but generally know that it's kind of a different technique that's used. And it doesn't cluster items. It just looks that the nearest neighbors to make a decision about what the label or what the outcome for a data point is going to be. So, the entire different things just want to put it out there that you don't get these two algorithms confused with each other. Questions so far? There are a lot of questions on Slido, but yes, question. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

children.title: K-Means Clustering and Inertia Explained
children.summary: K-Means clustering minimizes an objective function but may only find local minima due to random initialization and iterative movement, making it a local search algorithm. The difference between local and global optima is highlighted, referencing CS188. Inertia is presented as a loss function in K-means, with examples illustrating how different initial cluster centers can lead to varying local optima. The discussion also addresses the appropriateness of different clustering algorithms for various datasets and questions the uniformity of cluster radii.

children.title: K-Means Clustering and Inertia Explained
children.summary: K-Means clustering minimizes an objective function but may achieve only local minima due to random initialization and iterative movement, making it a local search algorithm. The distinction between local and global optima is emphasized, referencing CS188. Inertia is introduced as a loss function in K-means, with examples (A, B, and C) illustrating how different initial cluster centers can lead to varying local optima. The discussion also covers the suitability of different clustering algorithms for various datasets and questions the uniformity of cluster radii.

children.title: K-Means Clustering and Inertia Explained
children.summary: K-Means clustering aims to minimize an objective function, achieving local minima but not guaranteeing a global optimum due to its reliance on random initialization and iterative movement. This process is a local search algorithm, highlighting the importance of distinguishing between local and global optima, as discussed in CS188. Inertia is introduced as a loss function in K-means clustering, where the random initialization of cluster centers can lead to different local optimum solutions, demonstrated through examples labeled A, B, and C. The segment also addresses the appropriateness of various clustering algorithms for different datasets and questions the uniformity of cluster radii.

children.title: K-Means Clustering and Local Optima
children.summary: K-Means clustering aims to minimize an objective function, achieving a minimum point; however, it does not guarantee finding the global optimum. The process involves random initialization and iterative movement, functioning as a local search algorithm. This concept is related to techniques taught in CS188, emphasizing the importance of understanding local versus global optima in clustering.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Does Kamin always convert? Was that a question? I think it will converge and then we're going to take a look at the metric that we're going to use in order to measure camions or measure the quality of camions and because it is always trying to minimize a certain objective function, there is a minimum point that it will achieve. But the issue is that came is not guaranteed to find the global optimum. Every time that you saw us randomly initialize something and then make iterative movement, think about that as a local search algorithm, that's a good thing that you will learn in CS188. If you take 188, I tell students that you will learn a lot of like... need techniques that other courses might not teach you from one aviate so that's a that's a good course to take some time but Because it's a local search algorithm and there is no direct solution for us actually finding the optimum number of clusters and then finding the optimum placement for the clusters. We're not guaranteed to reach the global optimum. But we will reach some stable point. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

leaf_chunk.audio: Does Kamin always convert? Was that a question? I think it will converge and then we're going to take a look at the metric that we're going to use in order to measure camions or measure the quality of camions and because it is always trying to minimize a certain objective function, there is a minimum point that it will achieve. But the issue is that came is not guaranteed to find the global optimum. Every time that you saw us randomly initialize something and then make iterative movement, think about that as a local search algorithm, that's a good thing that you will learn in CS188. If you take 188, I tell students that you will learn a lot of like... need techniques that other courses might not teach you from one aviate so that's a that's a good course to take some time but Because it's a local search algorithm and there is no direct solution for us actually finding the optimum number of clusters and then finding the optimum placement for the clusters. We're not guaranteed to reach the global optimum. But we will reach some stable point.

children.title: Understanding Inertia in Clustering
children.summary: Inertia is discussed as a loss function in clustering, specifically in the context of K-means clustering. The speaker explains the concept of initializing cluster centers randomly and how different initial seeds can lead to varying local optimum solutions, illustrated by multiple clustering outcomes labeled A, B, and C. The discussion also touches on the suitability of different clustering algorithms for various datasets and poses a question regarding the uniformity of cluster radii.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Good question. So let's not talk about this loss function. I feel like I'm the loss function person except for I think Joey taught us entropy loss. But let's take a look at this inertia and build the intuition behind why inertia is a loss function that came in clustering is trying to optimize. So say if I pick the same data set that we have before and I go by randomly saying that I want to put four clusters, right? And I randomly initialize the placement of four different center of clusters, and then at the end of the algorithm, I get those four clusters. Let's assume that I go ahead and redo exactly the same thing multiple times with different random initializations. So the seed was set to be 25 then 29 then 40, so that's why the initializations are different. That's why depending on the initialization, you might land into at different local optimum solutions, right? So you're seeing ABC as different kinds of clustering that we're doing. Take a look at them, which one do you like better? That's the first slide of the day. There's good question about whether the radius of those clusters are the same. We're going to talk about the issue it came in the clustering and why we're going to see a and that you're a different clustering algorithm. And we'll give you intuition about. In different cases handling different datasets. Which one of them is going to be more suitable? Video: 1: V

leaf_chunk.audio: Good question. So let's not talk about this loss function. I feel like I'm the loss function person except for I think Joey taught us entropy loss. But let's take a look at this inertia and build the intuition behind why inertia is a loss function that came in clustering is trying to optimize. So say if I pick the same data set that we have before and I go by randomly saying that I want to put four clusters, right? And I randomly initialize the placement of four different center of clusters, and then at the end of the algorithm, I get those four clusters. Let's assume that I go ahead and redo exactly the same thing multiple times with different random initializations. So the seed was set to be 25 then 29 then 40, so that's why the initializations are different. That's why depending on the initialization, you might land into at different local optimum solutions, right? So you're seeing ABC as different kinds of clustering that we're doing. Take a look at them, which one do you like better? That's the first slide of the day. There's good question about whether the radius of those clusters are the same. children.title: Evaluating Clustering Metrics and Choosing Optimal K
children.summary: The discussion covers evaluating clustering options for K equal to four, highlighting the visual appeal of option A over B and C. It emphasizes the importance of establishing a loss function or metric for assessing clustering quality, introducing key evaluation metrics such as inertia, minimum distance, mean shortest distance, weighted sum of square distance, RMS, standard deviation, and silhouette score. Inertia, defined as the sum of squared distances from each data point to its cluster center, is a critical loss function in K-Means clustering, minimized through adjustments of cluster centers. An example illustrates inertia and distortion calculations, revealing variability across different K-Means runs. The challenges of optimizing inertia in payments optimization due to its NP-hard nature are discussed, along with a local search algorithm for optimizing inertia that requires restarts from different initialization points. The segment also addresses the selection of the optimal number of clusters, K, influenced by various factors, including the use of a dendrogram to identify the largest vertical gap and the systematic approach of performing a parameter search by plotting inertia against K. The Elbow Method is specifically mentioned for K-Means clustering, where the 'elbow' point suggests the optimal K, although complex datasets may not always show a clear elbow.

children.title: Evaluating Clustering Metrics and Inertia in K-Means
children.summary: The discussion focuses on evaluating clustering options for K equal to four, highlighting the visual appeal of option A over B and C. It emphasizes the importance of establishing a loss function or metric for quantitatively assessing clustering quality, introducing key evaluation metrics such as inertia, minimum distance, mean shortest distance, weighted sum of square distance, RMS, standard deviation, and silhouette score. Inertia, defined as the sum of squared distances from each data point to its cluster center, is identified as a critical loss function in K-Means clustering, where it is minimized through adjustments of cluster centers. An example illustrates calculations of inertia and distortion, revealing variability across different K-Means runs. The segment also addresses the challenges of optimizing inertia in payments optimization due to the NP-hard nature of the problem. Additionally, it covers the local search algorithm for optimizing inertia, emphasizing the need to restart from different initialization points to improve solution quality.

children.title: Evaluating Clustering with Loss Functions and Metrics
children.summary: A discussion on evaluating clustering options for K equal to four highlights the visual appeal of option A compared to B and C. The necessity of establishing a loss function or metric to quantitatively assess clustering quality is emphasized, prompting questions about measuring clustering results' goodness. Key evaluation metrics include inertia, defined as the sum of squared distances from each data point to the cluster center, as well as minimum distance, mean shortest distance, weighted sum of square distance, RMS, standard deviation, and silhouette score. These metrics are crucial for assessing cluster quality by measuring distances from the center and considering cluster shapes and sizes.

children.title: Evaluating Clustering Options with Loss Functions
children.summary: A discussion on evaluating clustering outputs for K equal to four, focusing on why option A is visually more appealing than options B and C. The importance of establishing a loss function or metric to quantitatively assess the quality of clustering is emphasized, prompting consideration of how to measure the goodness of the clustering results.

What kind of loss function or what kind of metric do we want to use so that we say that, okay, option A was good and option B and C were not as good. leaf_chunk.audio: You're good. I mean, I wonder if we have 150 folks before Thanksgiving. Yay, we do. Okay, let's see. Most of you picked A and you thought that A is a better clustering for K equal to four. There's one thing that better K equal to four was necessary at all or not. But just hypothetically, let's say I wanted to do K equal to four, looking at all of those clusters, it's kind of natural that A is a better option. But in order to decide why A was visually more appealing than B and C, we need to come up with some notion of a loss function. What kind of loss function or what kind of metric do we want to use so that we say that, okay, option A was good and option B and C were not as good. So how can we actually quantitatively measure the goodness of A and help that option C is?

children.title: Evaluation Metrics for Clustering Analysis
children.summary: Discussion on evaluation metrics for clustering, focusing on the concept of distance in clustering, including distance to important points and other points within the same cluster. The importance of defining a loss function for clustering is emphasized, questioning what makes one clustering result better or worse than another.

children.title: Loss Functions and Evaluation Metrics in Clustering
children.summary: To evaluate different clustering results, a loss function is necessary. Inertia is a key metric, defined as the sum of squared distances from each data point to the cluster center. Other evaluation metrics include minimum distance, mean shortest distance, weighted sum of square distance, RMS, standard deviation, and silhouette score. These metrics help assess the quality of clusters by measuring distances from the center and considering cluster shapes and sizes.

Two common loss functions:
Inertia: Sum of squared distances from each data point to its center.
Distortion: Weighted sum of squared distances from each data point to its center.

children.title: Inertia, Loss Functions, and K-Means Clustering
children.summary: Inertia is defined as the sum of squared distances from each data point to its cluster center, penalizing large but coherent clusters. Averaging these distances can enhance clustering evaluation. Two common loss functions for clustering are inertia and distortion, with distortion being a weighted sum of squared distances. In K-Means clustering, inertia is the loss function minimized, involving steps to adjust cluster centers and assign points to the nearest center. An example illustrates inertia and distortion calculations, showing variability in inertia across different K-Means runs. The discussion also covers inertia values in payments optimization, noting challenges in finding a global optimum due to the NP-hard nature of the problem and the limitations of K-Means in achieving global optimization.

children.title: Inertia and Loss Functions in Clustering
children.summary: Inertia is defined as the sum of squared distances from each data point to its cluster center, which can penalize large but coherent clusters. To improve clustering evaluation, averaging these squared distances by the number of data points in the cluster can be beneficial. This method emphasizes larger deviations due to the nature of squared distances, encouraging points to be closer to the center and resulting in more coherent clusters. Two common loss functions for evaluating clustering results are inertia and distortion, with distortion being a weighted sum of squared distances.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then I call this inertia. One issue that inertia has is that it is going to penalize large clusters, large but coherent clusters. What we can also do is we can also say that, okay, what if I go ahead and do evaded some of all of those squared distances, meaning that I'm going to divide each one of those sum of squared distances by the number of data points that I have in that cluster. So then average I look at what is the average square distance of the points to the center of the cluster. Why are we looking at this squared again? By this point, it should be super trivial. Why are we not looking at just the distance? As a single value. Ember scoring the distance. Ideas We are thinner than last right? The distance is always a positive value. So if you're just looking at the lengths distance so it can be... the notion of L1 and L2, kind of norm that we had, right? So, a squared distance is are going to penalize larger deviations more. And then therefore, it tries to actually bring everything closer to the center. And because it brings everything to the center, then it actually tries to have coherent clusters that way. leaf_chunk.audio: And then I call this inertia. One issue that inertia has is that it is going to penalize large clusters, large but coherent clusters. What we can also do is we can also say that, okay, what if I go ahead and do evaded some of all of those squared distances, meaning that I'm going to divide each one of those sum of squared distances by the number of data points that I have in that cluster. So then average I look at what is the average square distance of the points to the center of the cluster. Why are we looking at this squared again? By this point, it should be super trivial. Why are we not looking at just the distance? As a single value. Ember scoring the distance. Ideas We are thinner than last right? The distance is always a positive value. So if you're just looking at the lengths distance so it can be... the notion of L1 and L2, kind of norm that we had, right? So, a squared distance is are going to penalize larger deviations more. And then therefore, it tries to actually bring everything closer to the center. And because it brings everything to the center, then it actually tries to have coherent clusters that way.

children.title: Inertia and Distortion in K-Means Clustering
children.summary: Inertia serves as the loss function optimized by K-Means clustering, aiming to minimize the distance between data points and their respective centers. The process involves two steps: moving the center towards the data points and assigning points to the nearest center, both of which focus on minimizing inertia. Two common loss functions are defined: Inertia, which is the sum of squared distances from each data point to its center, and Distortion, a weighted sum of these distances. An example illustrates the calculations for inertia and distortion using specific data points, highlighting that different runs of K-Means can yield varying inertia values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, this is our last function, inertia, distortion. It turns out that the inertia is the loss function that is being optimized by K-Mins clustering because of the way that it tries to move the center to the center of the data points. It tries to minimize inertia there and then at the point that it tries to assign data points to the closest center. So it tries to also figure out which one of the which one of the centers is closer so in both of those the steps it tries to minimize some notion or some factor related to inertia. leaf_chunk.audio: So, this is our last function, inertia, distortion. It turns out that the inertia is the loss function that is being optimized by K-Mins clustering because of the way that it tries to move the center to the center of the data points. It tries to minimize inertia there and then at the point that it tries to assign data points to the closest center. So it tries to also figure out which one of the which one of the centers is closer so in both of those the steps it tries to minimize some notion or some factor related to inertia.

children.title: Inertia and Global Optimum in Payments Optimization
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the inertia values for different cases, noting that the first option had an inertia of 44, the second 45, and the rightmost option 54, indicating it was significantly worse. Payments aim to optimize inertia, but due to the NP-hard nature of the problem, finding a global optimum is challenging. A local search algorithm is typically employed for incremental optimization. The limitations of the K-Means clustering algorithm are highlighted, as it minimizes inertia in each step but struggles to find the global optimum due to two optimizers: one that holds the center position constant while allocating points to clusters, and another that keeps data colors constant while optimizing center positions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: If I go ahead and take a look at the inertia for all of those cases you would see that the inertia for the first one was 44 inertia for the second one was 45 so it was very close but the inertia for the right most one was 54 so that's why it was substantially worse than the first option that we had seen. So what's happening with the payments and inertia is that as I told you, payments is optimizing inertia. But the issue is that payments is not going to be able to most of the cases find the global optimum. And the reason is that this problem is NP-hard solving this problem is very difficult directly. And that's why we always throw in some kind of a local search algorithm in order to incrementally optimize and get to the optimum point. But then we had an algorithm. Why wasn't this came in algorithm able to get us to the optimum? Because it was minimizing inertia in every step, every step of those, in every iteration that we had two steps, each one of those two steps, we validated that it tries to minimize some notion of the inertia. But the issue is that why isn't it effective in terms of finding the global optimum? And the issue is that we have two optimizers that none of these two optimizers is taking the full control. The first optimizer that we had holds the center position constant, and then it tries to only allocate points to the right cluster. And then the second optimizer is actually holding the data colors constant, but then it actually tries to now optimize the center positions. So there are two things that needs to be optimizers. leaf_chunk.audio: If I go ahead and take a look at the inertia for all of those cases you would see that the inertia for the first one was 44 inertia for the second one was 45 so it was very close but the inertia for the right most one was 54 so that's why it was substantially worse than the first option that we had seen. So what's happening with the payments and inertia is that as I told you, payments is optimizing inertia. But the issue is that payments is not going to be able to most of the cases find the global optimum. And the reason is that this problem is NP-hard solving this problem is very difficult directly. And that's why we always throw in some kind of a local search algorithm in order to incrementally optimize and get to the optimum point. But then we had an algorithm. Why wasn't this came in algorithm able to get us to the optimum? Because it was minimizing inertia in every step, every step of those, in every iteration that we had two steps, each one of those two steps, we validated that it tries to minimize some notion of the inertia. But the issue is that why isn't it effective in terms of finding the global optimum? And the issue is that we have two optimizers that none of these two optimizers is taking the full control. The first optimizer that we had holds the center position constant, and then it tries to only allocate points to the right cluster. And then the second optimizer is actually holding the data colors constant, but then it actually tries to now optimize the center positions. So there are two things that needs to be optimizers.
