children.title: K-Means Clustering and Inertia Optimization
children.summary: K-Means clustering aims to minimize a function known as inertia, which often leads to challenges in finding the global optimum. The process can be viewed as two optimizers that alternate roles: the first optimizer keeps the center positions constant while optimizing the data colors, and the second optimizer holds the data colors constant while optimizing the center positions. This sequential approach can hinder the ability to achieve the best clustering solution.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The coloring thing that needs to be optimized, and there is also the center position that needs to be optimized. And because these two steps are kind of sequential to each other and they're not taking a full control of those of those variables, then they're not able to find the global optimum for us. But what's the one way that we can actually solve it and find the direct optimum solution for this kind of clustering task? And this is a very hard problem. So let's assume that hypothetically, I'm not concerned with the runtime. I can just go ahead and I have end different data points. I want to cluster them into K different clusters and K is picked in advance as you know. If I'm not worried about the runtime, how can I get to the optimum, optimum solution of K-means? children.title: K-means Clustering and NP-hardness
children.summary: An optimum solution for K-means clustering involves assigning each data point to any of the k clusters, leading to k options for each data point. This can be framed as a coloring problem where the number of possible colorings is k to the power of n (k^n). The process includes evaluating all colorings to find cluster centers and calculating inertia for each configuration. Due to the exponential growth of possibilities as n increases, this problem is classified as NP-hard, making direct solutions infeasible.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: An optimum, optimum solution of K-means is that I just need to make sure that if I'm considering that every data point can have, can be assigned to any cluster that it wants, right? And by any cluster that it wants, that means that, for example, a data point that we're looking, 0.1 can be red or can be green or can be orange and data.2 can also select all of those options. For every given data point, we have k options, k option for data.1, k option for data.2, k option for data.3, and then so on and so forth for k options for data.n. So we can reduce this clustering kind of problem into a coloring problem where we're just not concerned with the the center is anymore, and we're just going to color each one of the data points that we have. The number of options that we have here is k to the n number of colorings that we can have, because again, every data point can have, it can take different k different colors and be assigned to any cluster that they want. So what we'll do is we will look at all of these colorings, we will actually go through a loop for all of these colorings, what we'll do is we'll go ahead and find the cluster center of each one of those colors because a full coloring is there. So it just needs to find the center of all of those clusters. Once you find the center, you can actually calculate the inertia. Once you calculate the inertia, then you can actually keep track of what is the best inertia that you had seen across all of these K to the end potential colorings. And because K to the N is an exponential runtime, that's why this problem is hard to solve, is categorized as an NP-hard problem that we can solve it directly. Because as N increases, this is an exponential runtime that we can actually do. And the only way that we can actually find is pseudo. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

leaf_chunk.audio: An optimum, optimum solution of K-means is that I just need to make sure that if I'm considering that every data point can have, can be assigned to any cluster that it wants, right? And by any cluster that it wants, that means that, for example, a data point that we're looking, 0.1 can be red or can be green or can be orange and data.2 can also select all of those options. For every given data point, we have k options, k option for data.1, k option for data.2, k option for data.3, and then so on and so forth for k options for data.n. So we can reduce this clustering kind of problem into a coloring problem where we're just not concerned with the the center is anymore, and we're just going to color each one of the data points that we have. The number of options that we have here is k to the n number of colorings that we can have, because again, every data point can have, it can take different k different colors and be assigned to any cluster that they want. So what we'll do is we will look at all of these colorings, we will actually go through a loop for all of these colorings, what we'll do is we'll go ahead and find the cluster center of each one of those colors because a full coloring is there. So it just needs to find the center of all of those clusters. Once you find the center, you can actually calculate the inertia. Once you calculate the inertia, then you can actually keep track of what is the best inertia that you had seen across all of these K to the end potential colorings. And because K to the N is an exponential runtime, that's why this problem is hard to solve, is categorized as an NP-hard problem that we can solve it directly. Because as N increases, this is an exponential runtime that we can actually do. And the only way that we can actually find is pseudo.

children.title: Optimizing Inertia in K-Means Clustering
children.summary: The segment covers the local search algorithm for optimizing inertia in k-means clustering, emphasizing the need to restart the algorithm from different initialization points to enhance solution quality. It details the process of initializing with k centers, finding the optimum solution, and evaluating inertia. An algorithm is proposed that computes all possible colorings and their corresponding centers, updating the best-known centers and coloring when a lower inertia is achieved. Additionally, it addresses issues with k-means clustering, including mislabeling of data points and the challenge of optimizing inertia, noting that minimizing exactly in k-means is NP-hard. The importance of inertia as a measure of cluster quality is discussed, highlighting that K-Means may misrepresent ideal clustering configurations.

children.title: Optimizing Inertia in K-Means Clustering
children.summary: The segment discusses the local search algorithm for optimizing inertia in k-means clustering. It highlights the importance of restarting the algorithm from different initialization points to improve the solution quality. The process involves initializing with k centers, finding the optimum solution, and evaluating inertia. An algorithm is proposed to optimize inertia for a given k, where it computes all possible colorings and their corresponding centers, updating the best known centers and coloring when a better inertia is found. The segment concludes by noting that minimizing exactly in k-means is an NP-hard problem.

children.title: Issues with K-Means Clustering and Inertia Optimization
children.summary: K-Means clustering can lead to mislabeling of data points, as demonstrated by an example where a point closer to an orange cluster was incorrectly labeled as blue. The algorithm's inertia values can sometimes misrepresent the ideal clustering, with one configuration showing an inertia of 87 while a visually preferable one had an inertia of 94. The challenge of optimizing inertia for a given K involves finding the best centers and colorings without worrying about runtime, as the problem is NP-hard. An algorithm is proposed that evaluates all possible K colorings, computes the corresponding K centers, and updates the best-known configuration if a better inertia is found.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then you keep track of which inertia or which clustering was the minimum. So that's kind of the only way or one of the ways that we can actually optimize it a little bit further, but we cannot actually run this exponential time runtime. So, with that kind of mechanic and explanation out of the way, I want to now talk a little bit about the issues with Cayman's clustering. So take a look at what's happening if I run Cayman's clustering. So when I run Cayman's clustering, remember that I only, at the point that the algorithm converged, I had this single data point that was closer to the orange cluster as opposed to the blue cluster, but it was still labeled as blue. But the ideal scenario is that we probably want something close to this left one. And this is the issue that we'll have with with Kameh's clustering because Kameh's clustering says that okay the inertia on this one is 87 and the inertia on this one that visually is more appealing to us is 94. children.title: K-Means Clustering and Inertia Discussion
children.summary: The segment discusses the comparison between two clustering results using K-Means, emphasizing the importance of inertia as a measure of cluster quality. It highlights that while one clustering may visually appear better, the underlying issue is that K-Means focuses solely on distances between data points and cluster centers, neglecting the separation or 'blobbiness' of the clusters. The speaker indicates that a new algorithm will be introduced to address these shortcomings by incorporating a new loss function that considers the distribution of data points within clusters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There was one slide that we can actually have, which one is better. I assume you all will say A is better, but if you haven't responded to previous polls, feel free to respond, and then I'll move on to explaining what the issue is exactly and how to solve it. So why is the right one better or the left one better in our opinion as opposed to the right one and why inertia and the right one that came in's clustering is giving us is is lower than the inertia and the one that is visually more appealing to us. The issue is that camions clustering is not going to consider, is only concerned with the distances. It's not concerned with the blobbyness of each one of those clusters. So for example, in this case, it says that, oh, this data point belongs to the cluster blue because it is closer to this center as opposed to this other center. But it doesn't consider the fact that we want to have a complete separation between the clusters and we're not kind of in an ideal situation if a single tone data point is actually close to an irrelevant class the right over there. So that's why that's the thing that came in as clustering is not considering. Whether that's wrong or not, that's the separate story, but we're actually trying to find a new algorithm that that new algorithm tries to solve this issue a little bit, and then it tries to consider blobbing us or how well cluster data points are to the closest cluster that they're in using some new loss function that we're going to talk about. leaf_chunk.audio: There was one slide that we can actually have, which one is better. I assume you all will say A is better, but if you haven't responded to previous polls, feel free to respond, and then I'll move on to explaining what the issue is exactly and how to solve it. So why is the right one better or the left one better in our opinion as opposed to the right one and why inertia and the right one that came in's clustering is giving us is is lower than the inertia and the one that is visually more appealing to us. The issue is that camions clustering is not going to consider, is only concerned with the distances. It's not concerned with the blobbyness of each one of those clusters. So for example, in this case, it says that, oh, this data point belongs to the cluster blue because it is closer to this center as opposed to this other center. But it doesn't consider the fact that we want to have a complete separation between the clusters and we're not kind of in an ideal situation if a single tone data point is actually close to an irrelevant class the right over there. So that's why that's the thing that came in as clustering is not considering. Whether that's wrong or not, that's the separate story, but we're actually trying to find a new algorithm that that new algorithm tries to solve this issue a little bit, and then it tries to consider blobbing us or how well cluster data points are to the closest cluster that they're in using some new loss function that we're going to talk about.

children.title: Introduction to Clustering and K-means Method
children.summary: Clustering is introduced as a fundamental and practical topic in research, with a lab session dedicated to it and no associated homework. The k-means clustering method is highlighted for its relevance. Hierarchical clustering is explained as a bottom-up approach where each data point starts as a separate cluster, merging based on proximity until the desired number of clusters is reached. The merging decision relies on a linkage criterion, specifically the single linkage criterion, which measures the distance between clusters using the closest data points.

children.title: Break Before Discussing New Algorithm
children.summary: A two-minute break is announced before transitioning to a discussion on a new algorithm that utilizes a bottom-up approach or a plumb rate approach.

children.title: Introduction to Clustering and K-means
children.summary: Clustering is presented as a fundamental topic that is easier to grasp and highly useful in research applications. A lab session focused on clustering will be included in the course, although there will be no homework associated with it. The k-means clustering method is briefly mentioned, indicating its relevance in the upcoming discussions.

children.title: Hierarchical Clustering and Linkage Criteria
children.summary: The hierarchical approach to clustering involves starting with each data point as a separate cluster and merging them based on their proximity until the desired number of clusters is achieved. This method is known as a bottom-up or collaborative approach. The decision on which clusters to merge is determined by a linkage criterion. The single linkage criterion specifically measures the distance between clusters based on the closest data points in each cluster, allowing for the merging of clusters that contain the most similar data points.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So let's see this hierarchical approach. So with this hierarchical approach, we've seen the issue that we have before. That the issue was that if you're using some notion of inertia that only looks at the distances, without considering the closeness of the data points to the right cluster, that's going to be concerning. What we can actually do is we can actually go ahead assume that all of the data points are in different clusters. And then the closest points to each other or the proposes clusters to each other will go ahead and combine them together upon to the point that we build enough clusters that we want. So in this approach what we'll do as I as I said before is that we'll start with all of the data points as a separate cluster. We'll keep merging similar clusters or data points to each other up until the point that we have enough clusters that we want and that's why it's called bottom up approach or a collaborative approach. The way that we decide which two clusters or two data points are going to be connected to each other is called linkage criterion. And then the way that we're going to talk about this linkage criterion is that we can decide about our own policy of how we're going to combine these clusters together. There is the single linkage criteria that the single linkage criteria says that look at two clusters and these two clusters at distance between these two clusters is the distance of the closest data points of these two clusters to each other. other. So it basically says that we are looking at the similarity of the most similar data points in two clusters. So as long as the two data points that are closest to each other and those two clusters are close to each other, then those two clusters are going to be merged to each other. work. We have eight data points, eight clusters to start, but what we can actually look at is we're looking at the distance of each one of these clusters to other clusters that we have. In this case, it's very simple because our clusters are only single data point clusters. What I can actually do is I can say that the distance between these two clusters is this line, the distance between this cluster and this cluster is this line, the distance between this cluster and this cluster is this line. But again, we're going to go with the priority of merging the closest clusters to each other. So whether the candidate closes clusters, again the candidate closes clusters are these pairs that we're seeing right here. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

leaf_chunk.audio: So let's see this hierarchical approach. So with this hierarchical approach, we've seen the issue that we have before. That the issue was that if you're using some notion of inertia that only looks at the distances, without considering the closeness of the data points to the right cluster, that's going to be concerning. What we can actually do is we can actually go ahead assume that all of the data points are in different clusters. And then the closest points to each other or the proposes clusters to each other will go ahead and combine them together upon to the point that we build enough clusters that we want. So in this approach what we'll do as I as I said before is that we'll start with all of the data points as a separate cluster. We'll keep merging similar clusters or data points to each other up until the point that we have enough clusters that we want and that's why it's called bottom up approach or a collaborative approach. The way that we decide which two clusters or two data points are going to be connected to each other is called linkage criterion. And then the way that we're going to talk about this linkage criterion is that we can decide about our own policy of how we're going to combine these clusters together. There is the single linkage criteria that the single linkage criteria says that look at two clusters and these two clusters at distance between these two clusters is the distance of the closest data points of these two clusters to each other. other. So it basically says that we are looking at the similarity of the most similar data points in two clusters. So as long as the two data points that are closest to each other and those two clusters are close to each other, then those two clusters are going to be merged to each other. work. We have eight data points, eight clusters to start, but what we can actually look at is we're looking at the distance of each one of these clusters to other clusters that we have. In this case, it's very simple because our clusters are only single data point clusters. What I can actually do is I can say that the distance between these two clusters is this line, the distance between this cluster and this cluster is this line, the distance between this cluster and this cluster is this line. But again, we're going to go with the priority of merging the closest clusters to each other. So whether the candidate closes clusters, again the candidate closes clusters are these pairs that we're seeing right here.

children.title: Single Linkage and Cluster Merging in Analysis
children.summary: The segment covers single linkage in cluster analysis, defining the distance between clusters as the minimum distance between points in each. It provides an example comparing a two-point cluster to a single point, emphasizing the significance of the closest distance for cluster similarity. The process of merging clusters is explained, focusing on evaluating distances between clusters and identifying the closest points for merging. It also discusses linkage criteria in hierarchical clustering, introducing three types: single linkage (minimum distance), average linkage (average distance), and complete linkage (maximum distance), with options to cluster until a desired number is reached or to stop early.

children.title: Single Linkage in Cluster Analysis
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of single linkage in clustering, emphasizing that the distance between two clusters is determined by the minimum distance between points in each cluster. An example is provided where a cluster of two points is compared to a single point, highlighting the importance of measuring the closest distance to establish similarity between clusters.

children.title: Single Linkage and Distance Criteria in Clustering
children.summary: The segment discusses the criteria for determining distances between clusters and data points in clustering. It explains the concept of single linkage, where the distance between two clusters is defined as the minimum distance between any point in the first cluster and any point in the second. An example illustrates how to identify the closest point in a cluster to a given data point.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That's the criteria that we're looking. The distance between that cluster with two points and this data point right here again. children.title: Cluster Merging and Distance Calculation
children.summary: The process of merging clusters involves evaluating the distances between them, focusing on the closest points. The next candidate for merging is determined by comparing the distances of the top and bottom clusters, identifying the minimum distance rather than diagonal distances. The top row clusters are combined first based on their shorter distance, followed by the bottom row clusters, continuing this process until all clusters merge into one.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Convince that all of those red distances are closer. So we'll keep combining those pairs and we'll not look at any other distances up until the point that any of them can actually be meaningful or can be the closest kind of distance between two clusters that we can combine. So I'm going to go ahead remove these ones and then we're going to find the next candidate. So now looking at the distances between these four clusters that I have. So what's the distance between the two top clusters that I have? the distance between the closest points. So it's going to be the distance between these two. Same thing also for the bottom two clusters for the top row and the bottom row between these two clusters. The distance is going to be one of these distances that is going to be minimum as opposed to diagonal distance because that's closer and also the same thing here. here and obviously this cluster and this cluster are far from each other but the minimum distance between them is this line. So of all of these combinations which one of those lines looks shorter to you? either the top row or the bottom row. So we will go ahead and combine the top row clusters first, and then the bottom row clusters second, and then we're going to combine everything up until a point that we have one cluster. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

children.title: Linkage Criteria in Clustering Methods
children.summary: Different approaches for merging clusters in hierarchical clustering are discussed, focusing on linkage criteria. The segment explains that one can either cluster until a desired number of clusters is reached or stop early if the number is known. Three main types of linkage criteria are introduced: single linkage, which measures the minimum distance between points in two clusters; average linkage, which considers the average distance between all pairs of points in the clusters; and complete linkage, which looks at the maximum distance between points in the clusters to ensure that the farthest points are close before merging, promoting a more cohesive cluster formation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So there is one thing that either you will go all the way up until a point that you have one cluster, and then later on come down and then figure out how many clusters you want to have. So you will break it up to, for example, three steps, and then get your tree clusters that you want it. or you can stop at 3 if you know ahead of time that your case equal to 3 so you don't need to go all the way to 1. So there are different kinds of, as I said, linkage criteria, which there is another version that looks at the average distances as opposed to the closest distances that's called average linkage. There is also a complete linkage that looks at the maximum distances between the clusters. That essentially tries to make sure that the two far parts of the clusters are close to each other before merging them. So that kind of enforces a little bit of blobbyness in a sense that came in clustering was not giving us. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

leaf_chunk.audio: So there is one thing that either you will go all the way up until a point that you have one cluster, and then later on come down and then figure out how many clusters you want to have. So you will break it up to, for example, three steps, and then get your tree clusters that you want it. or you can stop at 3 if you know ahead of time that your case equal to 3 so you don't need to go all the way to 1. So there are different kinds of, as I said, linkage criteria, which there is another version that looks at the average distances as opposed to the closest distances that's called average linkage. There is also a complete linkage that looks at the maximum distances between the clusters. That essentially tries to make sure that the two far parts of the clusters are close to each other before merging them. So that kind of enforces a little bit of blobbyness in a sense that came in clustering was not giving us.

children.title: Cluster Merging Techniques and Agglomerative Clustering Overview
children.summary: The segment covers cluster merging techniques, focusing on complete linkage, where the distance between clusters is defined by the maximum distance between points in each cluster, illustrated with an example of eight clusters. It also briefly mentions single and average linkage as alternative methods. Additionally, it outlines the agglomerative clustering process, starting with 12 individual data points as clusters, calculating distances in a two-dimensional feature space, and merging the closest clusters, specifically noting clusters 10 and 11. The merging process is detailed with specific distances (0.14, 0.22, 0.3) and examples of merges, including points 10, 0, 4, and 1, 5, leading to larger clusters and further analysis of remaining distances for future merges.

children.title: Cluster Merging Techniques and Examples
children.summary: The segment discusses methods for merging clusters using different linkage criteria. It explains complete linkage, where the distance between clusters is determined by the maximum distance between points in each cluster, and illustrates this with an example involving eight clusters. The process of combining clusters is demonstrated, highlighting the distances and the rationale for choosing which clusters to merge. Additionally, the segment briefly mentions single linkage and average linkage as alternative methods for determining cluster similarity.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Again, let's look at an example. We have all of these eight points. We'll go ahead and combine all of these pairs first. And then if you're looking at the complete linkage, if I show you the distances and this kind of for for clusters that I have that distance between these two clusters is this line probably, this line probably because we're looking at the maximum distances. between this cluster top left and then the bottom right, the maximum distance is the far, the distance between the far data points, and then also from the top two clusters, this is the maximum distance, this is also the maximum distance, the far points. So given all of this, what is the next two clusters that should become bind? long. diagonal wise it's also very aligned between the top left one and the bottom right one. So it looks like that the top bottom directly is the best choice and then this is also the best choice and then we're combining it into one cluster. leaf_chunk.audio: Again, let's look at an example. We have all of these eight points. We'll go ahead and combine all of these pairs first. And then if you're looking at the complete linkage, if I show you the distances and this kind of for for clusters that I have that distance between these two clusters is this line probably, this line probably because we're looking at the maximum distances. between this cluster top left and then the bottom right, the maximum distance is the far, the distance between the far data points, and then also from the top two clusters, this is the maximum distance, this is also the maximum distance, the far points. So given all of this, what is the next two clusters that should become bind? long. diagonal wise it's also very aligned between the top left one and the bottom right one. So it looks like that the top bottom directly is the best choice and then this is also the best choice and then we're combining it into one cluster. So that's how we're going to progress for I'm having eight clusters and then going all the way to having only one cluster remain it. So one thing that we can actually do is see one more example on the same dataset.

children.title: Agglomerative Clustering Process Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses the initial steps of agglomerative clustering, where each of the 12 data points is assigned to its own cluster, labeled from ID 0 to ID 11. It highlights the process of calculating distances in a two-dimensional feature space and merging the closest clusters, specifically noting that clusters 10 and 11 have the shortest distance between them. The segment includes a visual representation of the data points and their distances.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You kind of see whether it is actually going to solve the issue that we had, but that single blue data point that we had near the entire kind of orange data set that we had. So I'm going to start with 12 data points, right? Initially I'm assigning each one of those data points to have a label for a single cluster. So I'm starting cluster ID zero all the way to to cluster ID 11. You can look at the distances. It's a two-dimensional feature space. What I'll do is I will incrementally show you the candidate distances that we have, and then we'll keep merging those two clusters together or the candidate clusters together. One thing that I want to show you initially is that, again, all of those labels are the same, but looking at the distances between, for example, 10 and 11, the legend here says that the distance is the shortest distance that we can have. What I'll do is I will incrementally show you the candidate distances that we have, and then we'll keep merging those two clusters together or the candidate clusters together. children.title: Agglomerative Clustering Process Steps
children.summary: The process of agglomerative clustering is demonstrated by merging clusters based on their distances. Initially, clusters are formed with individual data points, and the closest clusters are identified for merging. Distances between points are provided, with specific examples including distances of 0.14, 0.22, and 0.3. The first merge combines two points labeled with 10, followed by merging points 0 and 4, and then points 1 and 5, resulting in larger clusters. The remaining distances are analyzed to identify the next candidates for merging, specifically points 7 and 9.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The distance is 0.14. So those are the candidates to data points or two clusters that should be merged with each other. So what we're going to do is we're going to merge them. Now we have one cluster with two points labeled with 10. Now take a look at all of the rest of the annotations that I have. So I have the annotations for the distance between 0 and 4 to be 0.22. And then what you're seeing is the distance between 1 and 5 is also 0.22. And the distance between 7 and 9 is 0.3. And I didn't look at all of, I didn't annotate all of the distances because again, visually you can see that all of them are, the rest of them are not viable. I'm only showing the top tree lowest distances between the candidate clusters. So between 1 and 5 and 0 and 4, we can pick any of them and actually go ahead and combine them. Let's assume that we're going to go ahead and merge 0 and 4 with each other. So 0 and 4 are merged and now I have one cluster of 0. Okay, now you can see the remaining distances that I have. I still have 1 and 5 remaining. I still have 7 and 9 here and then I still have the distance between 6 and 8. And looking at the distances, again 1 and 5 is going to be my next candidate. So I'm going to go ahead and combine 1 and 5. And now I have one big cluster of one. So now I have one one cluster of one with two points one cluster of 10 with two points and then I have all of those other clusters right over there. And then the rest of the distances you can again see what is the next candidate that I can pick. So looking at the colors, the next candidate looks like that that's going to be seven and nine. leaf_chunk.audio: The distance is 0.14. So those are the candidates to data points or two clusters that should be merged with each other. So what we're going to do is we're going to merge them. Now we have one cluster with two points labeled with 10. Now take a look at all of the rest of the annotations that I have. So I have the annotations for the distance between 0 and 4 to be 0.22. And then what you're seeing is the distance between 1 and 5 is also 0.22. And the distance between 7 and 9 is 0.3. And I didn't look at all of, I didn't annotate all of the distances because again, visually you can see that all of them are, the rest of them are not viable. I'm only showing the top tree lowest distances between the candidate clusters. So between 1 and 5 and 0 and 4, we can pick any of them and actually go ahead and combine them. Let's assume that we're going to go ahead and merge 0 and 4 with each other. So 0 and 4 are merged and now I have one cluster of 0. Okay, now you can see the remaining distances that I have. I still have 1 and 5 remaining. I still have 7 and 9 here and then I still have the distance between 6 and 8. And looking at the distances, again 1 and 5 is going to be my next candidate. So I'm going to go ahead and combine 1 and 5. And now I have one big cluster of one. So now I have one one cluster of one with two points one cluster of 10 with two points and then I have all of those other clusters right over there. And then the rest of the distances you can again see what is the next candidate that I can pick. So looking at the colors, the next candidate looks like that that's going to be seven and nine.

children.title: Agglomerative Clustering: Complete Linkage and Distance Metrics
children.summary: The segment explains agglomerative clustering using complete linkage, focusing on the rationale for measuring distances between clusters. It highlights that the longest distance between clusters is used for merging decisions, with 10 clusters currently existing. The distance between cluster zero and the cluster tree is noted as 0.36. Clusters seven and nine are combined first due to their lowest distance of 0.30, followed by considerations for merging clusters one and ten (0.41) and six and eight. Key distances such as 0.30, 0.32, 0.36, 0.41, and 1.17 are presented, emphasizing the maximum distance among pairs for determining merges.

children.title: Agglomerative Clustering - Complete Linkage Explanation
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the rationale for determining the distance between the cluster tree and cluster zero using complete linkage. The longest line between the clusters is emphasized, illustrating the criteria for this method. At this point, there are 10 clusters, including 8 single-point clusters and 2 clusters with two points. The segment also presents the distances between clusters in hierarchical clustering progression, specifically addressing the distance between clusters 0 and 3, which is determined by the maximum distance among pairs.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But before I talk about that, I want to show you why I draw the line between for the distance between the cluster tree and cluster zero to be that below line instead of a line connecting the data point tree to the other zero. And that's because we're our criteria is that we're going to go with complete linkage. So between those two lines, the line that was drawn right here is the longest line. children.title: Agglomerative Clustering: Combining Clusters
children.summary: The distance between cluster zero and the cluster tree is noted as 0.36. When combining clusters seven and nine, which have the lowest distance of 0.30, a new cluster is formed. The next candidates for clustering include the distances between clusters one and ten (0.41) and between clusters six and eight. The process involves calculating distances and selecting clusters that are farther apart while ensuring that the far ends of the clusters remain close to each other, particularly considering the distance between cluster three and data points.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's why the distance between the cluster, zero and cluster tree right now is annotated to be equal to 0.36 that corresponds to this line that's shown. But if I go ahead and combine seven and nine, which has the lowest kind of distance that we're seeing point three, then I'll have one cluster with seven and seven right there. So what's the next cluster that I should next to clusters that I should come mine? And that's also a nice example. Look at the distance between cluster 1 and cluster 10. Finding the far points from cluster 10 and cluster 1 connecting them calculating the distance. It looks like that distance is 0.41 based on the color. But based on all of the other distances we're seeing looks like 6 and 8 is our next candidate. So I'm going to go ahead and combine 6 and 8. The next candidates are you can see that one in ten is a still a candidate zero and three is a still a candidate but now I also can consider the distance between three and six again notice that I pick the distance between three and this data point as opposed to this other data point because it was giving me a longer distance because I'm interested in actually Creating clusters that the far ends are also close to each other. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

children.title: Agglomerative Clustering - Complete Linkage Process
children.summary: The segment discusses the agglomerative clustering process using complete linkage. It identifies the next clusters to merge, specifically clusters 0 and 3, based on their distances. The distances between various clusters are presented, with key distances highlighted: 0.30, 0.32, 0.36, 0.41, and 1.17. The method emphasizes considering the maximum distance among pairs when determining merges.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So looking at what we have here, zero and tree looks to be the next candidate to become mine. Now I have one big cluster of tree, one cluster of seven, one cluster of six, one cluster of pen, one, and then a single data point for cluster two. Again, looking at what's happening here, it's very obvious that 10 and 1 needs to be connected to each other. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

children.title: Agglomerative Clustering and Distance Analysis
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of agglomerative clustering, specifically focusing on combining clusters based on distance metrics. It identifies the current number of clusters and emphasizes the importance of distance calculations, noting that the closest distance is between points 0 and 7. The segment explains how combining clusters alters distance metrics, particularly through complete linkage, and illustrates the connections between different clusters, such as cluster 2 and cluster 0. It highlights the need to evaluate distances between clusters to determine the appropriate number of clusters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we're connecting 10 and 1. Now I will have a big cluster of L1s, then I have a bunch of other distances to consider as the next step. So how many clusters do I have right now? One cluster, two, three, four, and five clusters. So you can again keep combining clusters up on the look point that you have enough number of clusters that you decided ahead of time or you can go all the way to one and then later on look at the composition of what's happening and then figure out which K was the What's the right number? So based on what we're seeing right here, looks like that the distance between 0 and 7 is the closest distance. You might also think that 0 and 6 is closer. The numbers are very close, but that's why I put the numbers because looking at the lines is not going to be very informative. So 0 and 7 are going to be combined. Now I have one big cluster of 0. Now look at how the distances are now changed because of the complete linkage. So now 2 is connected to the 4 point in the cluster 0. Now between the cluster 6 and cluster 2, the farthest points that I have is comparing this data point 6 and this data point in cluster 0 and then kind of similarly for comparing cluster 1 and cluster 6 as you can see. children.title: Agglomerative Clustering with Complete Linkage
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of agglomerative clustering using complete linkage, focusing on the combination of clusters 0 and 6, resulting in three remaining clusters. It emphasizes the importance of the bottom-up approach in preventing misclassification of data points, as points that are closer to each other are correctly clustered together, ensuring accurate representation of their relationships.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The closest distance again by considering even the complete linkage is going to be combining cluster 0 and cluster 6. We'll combine them. Now we have three clusters, yes, three clusters left and the top three distances are shown. So again, 0 and 2 are going to be combined here. And this is the important part. If we did not do it in the bottom-up approach, you remember what was happening with this data point that was closer to the center of these data points as opposed to the center of these data points and it was going to be misclassified or misclustered. In this case, it's not going to be the same because it was already clustered close to this other data point that it's closer to. So that was a done deal. It is going to go wherever that other data point is going to go. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin

leaf_chunk.audio: The closest distance again by considering even the complete linkage is going to be combining cluster 0 and cluster 6. We'll combine them. Now we have three clusters, yes, three clusters left and the top three distances are shown. So again, 0 and 2 are going to be combined here. And this is the important part. If we did not do it in the bottom-up approach, you remember what was happening with this data point that was closer to the center of these data points as opposed to the center of these data points and it was going to be misclassified or misclustered. In this case, it's not going to be the same because it was already clustered close to this other data point that it's closer to. So that was a done deal. It is going to go wherever that other data point is going to go.

children.title: Hierarchical Clustering and Dendrograms Overview
children.summary: The segment covers hierarchical clustering, focusing on the bottom-up approach where clusters evolve by combining zeros and ones into distinct groups. It highlights the significance of the clustering algorithm choice based on dataset shape and distribution. The discussion includes agglomerative clustering, which optimizes inertia and visualizes the clustering process through dendrograms—a tree structure showing how data points connect at various levels. An example illustrates cluster merging and the algorithm's effectiveness. Additionally, dendrograms are used to determine the optimal number of clusters (k) by identifying significant vertical gaps between horizontal lines, suggesting distinct clusters that should not be combined.

children.title: Hierarchical Clustering and Data Separation
children.summary: The discussion focuses on how clusters evolve through a bottom-up approach in hierarchical clustering, specifically mentioning the combination of zeros and ones into distinct clusters. It emphasizes that the choice of clustering algorithm can depend on the shape of the dataset, highlighting the importance of understanding data distribution in clustering techniques.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That's how kind of this blob is going to evolve and build on top of each other from kind of a bottom-up approach. So now in this case, zero and tree is going to be combined. Now I'm going to have one big cluster of all zeros and then one big cluster of all ones. And now you can see a full kind of separation between these two clusters. So again, depending on the shape of your data set, you can use different algorithms. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin

children.title: Agglomerative Clustering and Dendrograms
children.summary: The segment discusses agglomerative clustering, emphasizing its bottom-up approach to optimize inertia and find separations between clusters. It introduces the concept of a dendrogram, a tree structure that visually represents the clustering process, where data points are connected at various levels. As clusters merge, layers are added to the tree, ultimately leading to a single cluster at the top. An example illustrates how clusters combine, demonstrating the algorithm's effectiveness on the dataset.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Came in's clustering was optimizing inertia and then this approach is trying to actually find separation between the different clusters that we have and then it builds from a bottom up approach. If we apply to the entire data set, you can actually see that yes, this data point that we had issues with now is correctly clustered into the right cluster of blues. So there is the concept of dendrogram. And dendrogram is kind of a tree structure that shows you on the leaves. We have all of the data points that we have. It's like over plotted right here, but it can assume that all of the data points. And then at every point that two data points are connected to each other, or two clusters are connected to each other, it will connect them together and move to the higher level in the tree. So for example here, if we consider this entire green thing as one cluster, and we consider this black thing as another cluster, in one clustering step that we combine these two, we actually add one layer to the tree. And now we have another cluster, right? And then this cluster can then later on be merged with this other cluster right here, and that's how it's going to go up all the way to the case that we only have one cluster remaining at the top. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterini

leaf_chunk.audio: Came in's clustering was optimizing inertia and then this approach is trying to actually find separation between the different clusters that we have and then it builds from a bottom up approach. If we apply to the entire data set, you can actually see that yes, this data point that we had issues with now is correctly clustered into the right cluster of blues. So there is the concept of dendrogram. And dendrogram is kind of a tree structure that shows you on the leaves. We have all of the data points that we have. It's like over plotted right here, but it can assume that all of the data points. And then at every point that two data points are connected to each other, or two clusters are connected to each other, it will connect them together and move to the higher level in the tree. So for example here, if we consider this entire green thing as one cluster, and we consider this black thing as another cluster, in one clustering step that we combine these two, we actually add one layer to the tree. And now we have another cluster, right? And then this cluster can then later on be merged with this other cluster right here, and that's how it's going to go up all the way to the case that we only have one cluster remaining at the top.

children.title: Using Dendrograms to Determine Optimal Clusters
children.summary: Dendrograms visualize the order of cluster formation and can help identify the optimal number of clusters (k). By examining the horizontal lines in the dendrogram, one can see where clusters are combined. A significant vertical gap between these lines indicates that the clusters are distinct, suggesting they should not be combined. By identifying this gap, a line can be drawn to determine the optimal number of clusters, allowing for an empirical approach to selecting k without prior assumptions. Values of k, such as 2 or 3, may be suitable, while k equal to 1 is generally not advisable due to the observed vertical gap.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, dendrogram is kind of visualizing the order that these clusters are actually created in case you want to trace it back and in case you want to actually use this information later on. How can we actually use dendrogram in order to figure out what is the optimum number of k or the value for k? This is kind of a empirical kind of way of doing it, but effectively the idea is that if you look at the dendrogram, and you're looking at these horizontal lines, these horizontal lines are showing you the placement for the clusters and when those clusters are combined together. So for example, here it shows me that, okay, I had one cluster right here for all of these or all of those small data points that I had in the bottom right or bottom left of the graph. Then if I go ahead and cluster all of the data points in the top right part of the graph, and I go ahead and combine them together into one cluster, this is like H horizontal line is showing me the cluster creation. The idea is that if you look at the dendrogram and you're seeing a huge vertical gap between these horizontal lines, that shows that probably these two clusters did not align to each other. so the distance between them is huge, don't combine them. So what you will do is you identify that horizontal gap. and then you can actually place a line and you would say that at this line you would cut and you would not combine them any further. And in that case, what you can actually see is you can actually use this information to figure out how many clusters did you have at that point and the number of clusters that you have at that point is going to be the optimum. Number of clusters that you should use so you don't need to actually go as a priori and then decide what k is going to be but in this case, k equal to two or k equal to tree both are going to be. good values to pick for this purpose. And definitely K equal to one is not going to be good because there is this huge vertical gap that we're observing right here. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X
+
->

leaf_chunk.audio: So, dendrogram is kind of visualizing the order that these clusters are actually created in case you want to trace it back and in case you want to actually use this information later on. How can we actually use dendrogram in order to figure out what is the optimum number of k or the value for k? This is kind of a empirical kind of way of doing it, but effectively the idea is that if you look at the dendrogram, and you're looking at these horizontal lines, these horizontal lines are showing you the placement for the clusters and when those clusters are combined together. So for example, here it shows me that, okay, I had one cluster right here for all of these or all of those small data points that I had in the bottom right or bottom left of the graph. Then if I go ahead and cluster all of the data points in the top right part of the graph, and I go ahead and combine them together into one cluster, this is like H horizontal line is showing me the cluster creation. The idea is that if you look at the dendrogram and you're seeing a huge vertical gap between these horizontal lines, that shows that probably these two clusters did not align to each other. so the distance between them is huge, don't combine them. So what you will do is you identify that horizontal gap. and then you can actually place a line and you would say that at this line you would cut and you would not combine them any further. And in that case, what you can actually see is you can actually use this information to figure out how many clusters did you have at that point and the number of clusters that you have at that point is going to be the optimum. Number of clusters that you should use so you don't need to actually go as a priori and then decide what k is going to be but in this case, k equal to two or k equal to tree both are going to be. good values to pick for this purpose. And definitely K equal to one is not going to be good because there is this huge vertical gap that we're observing right here.
video:
Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

children.title: Choosing the Optimal K in Clustering Techniques
children.summary: Selecting the optimal number of clusters, K, is subjective and influenced by various factors. One method is using a dendrogram to find the largest vertical gap, where the number of vertical lines crossed by a horizontal line below this gap indicates the optimal K. Contextual factors, like geographic location, also play a role in the choice of K. A systematic approach involves performing a parameter search by calculating clustering for different K values and plotting inertia, which decreases with increasing K. The Elbow Method specifically applies to K-Means clustering, where inertia is plotted against K to find the 'elbow' point that suggests the optimal K. However, complex datasets may not always exhibit a clear elbow.

children.title: Choosing the Optimal K in Clustering Techniques
children.summary: Selecting the optimal number of clusters, K, is a subjective decision influenced by various factors. One method involves using a dendrogram to identify the largest vertical gap, indicating where clusters should not be merged. The number of vertical lines crossed by a horizontal line drawn below this gap represents the optimal K. The choice of K often depends on the context of the data, such as geographic location when clustering U.S. states. A systematic approach includes performing a parameter search by calculating clustering for various K values and plotting inertia, which decreases as K increases. The Elbow Method is a specific technique for K-Means clustering, where inertia is plotted against K values to find the 'elbow' point indicating optimal K. Complex datasets may not always show a clear elbow.

children.title: Choosing the Optimal K in Clustering
children.summary: Selecting the optimal number of clusters, K, is a subjective decision influenced by various factors. One method to determine K is by using a dendrogram, where the largest vertical gap between horizontal lines indicates a significant jump in distance, suggesting the point at which clusters should not be merged. By drawing a horizontal line below this gap, the number of vertical lines crossed represents the optimal K.

children.title: Choosing K for Clustering: Subjectivity in Decisions
children.summary: The choice of K in clustering algorithms is often subjective and depends on the context of the data being analyzed. For instance, when clustering U.S. states, one might consider various criteria such as geographic location or the number of clusters desired. The discussion highlights the importance of understanding how many clusters to create and the factors influencing this decision.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's a hyperparameter so you can also treat it as a subjective kind of hyperparameter that you're setting. So for example, if I'm looking at the states in the United States, and I want to cluster them, it's very subjective. Like how do you want to cluster all of these states? Do you want to cluster them based on the geographic location? Do you want to cluster them into four clusters? do you want to cluster them into two clusters, East and West, the states, or how do you want to actually do that? children.title: Choosing K in Clustering and Inertia Analysis
children.summary: Selecting the number of clusters, K, is subjective and depends on the specific use case. A systematic approach involves performing a parameter search by calculating clustering for various K values and plotting the corresponding inertia. Inertia, defined as the sum of squared distances to cluster centers, decreases as K increases because more clusters allow data points to be closer to a center, thus reducing the total distance. The goal is to identify the point of diminishing returns in inertia as K increases.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it's very subjective. So you pick a K based on the use case that you're dealing with. If you want to actually be more systematic, you can use something that we did also in PCA. Remember this script plot that we had, we also have the same thing here. We could actually say that go ahead and do parameter search and try different values of k. For different values of K, so here is the plot of different values of K, K, equal to one, two, three, and so on and so forth. Go ahead and calculate, do the clustering and calculate the inertia. And then kind of see what the inertia is as you're increasing the number of K. One thing that I want to mention is that definitely as the number of clusters increases, inertia decreases because inertia was not weighted, right? inertia was the sum of the squared distances to the cluster center, right? And that was actually for different clusters, it was calculated separately and then it will, it will add up. But effectively, if you have more cluster centers, then each one of those data points are going to have a better chance of being closer to a center, so that some of the squared distances is going to be a smaller. So therefore, the inertia is going to decrease as k increases. And what we're going to do is we're going to identify the point that we have the diminishing return afterwards. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

leaf_chunk.audio: So it's very subjective. So you pick a K based on the use case that you're dealing with. If you want to actually be more systematic, you can use something that we did also in PCA. Remember this script plot that we had, we also have the same thing here. We could actually say that go ahead and do parameter search and try different values of k. For different values of K, so here is the plot of different values of K, K, equal to one, two, three, and so on and so forth. Go ahead and calculate, do the clustering and calculate the inertia. And then kind of see what the inertia is as you're increasing the number of K. One thing that I want to mention is that definitely as the number of clusters increases, inertia decreases because inertia was not weighted, right? inertia was the sum of the squared distances to the cluster center, right? And that was actually for different clusters, it was calculated separately and then it will, it will add up. But effectively, if you have more cluster centers, then each one of those data points are going to have a better chance of being closer to a center, so that some of the squared distances is going to be a smaller. So therefore, the inertia is going to decrease as k increases. And what we're going to do is we're going to identify the point that we have the diminishing return afterwards.

children.title: Choosing K in K-Means Clustering
children.summary: The Elbow Method is discussed as a technique for selecting the number of clusters (K) in K-Means clustering. It involves plotting inertia against various K values and identifying the 'elbow' point where diminishing returns begin, indicating the optimal K. The example highlights that for K equal to 2 or 3, the dendrogram also supports this choice. Additionally, it notes that complex datasets may not exhibit a clear elbow.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So for example, in this case, we're seeing the diminishing return after k equal to 3. children.title: Choosing K in K-Means Clustering
children.summary: In K-Means clustering, increasing the number of clusters (K) reduces inertia because more cluster centers lead to smaller distances between data points and their nearest centers. However, optimizing for inertia by continuously increasing K can result in overfitting. The Elbow Method is a recommended approach to determine the optimal K by plotting inertia against various K values and identifying the 'elbow' point where diminishing returns occur. It's noted that complex datasets may not exhibit a clear elbow.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But if I go ahead and actually run k equal to 6, obviously the inertia is going to be less because what's happening is that I have more opportunities or more placement of these cluster centers. And by these cluster centers, cluster center placement is where distances are going to be smaller. Because right now, for example, this data point is going to be closer to this center as as opposed to this data point being compared against this center or another center. So the distances are going to be a little bit more reasonable. That's why what we would say is that inertia is not going to consider is Effectively, if you're trying to optimize inertia, it will actually, you will overfit the data. Because it will keep increasing K and then it will never end. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

leaf_chunk.audio: But if I go ahead and actually run k equal to 6, obviously the inertia is going to be less because what's happening is that I have more opportunities or more placement of these cluster centers. And by these cluster centers, cluster center placement is where distances are going to be smaller. Because right now, for example, this data point is going to be closer to this center as as opposed to this data point being compared against this center or another center. So the distances are going to be a little bit more reasonable. That's why what we would say is that inertia is not going to consider is Effectively, if you're trying to optimize inertia, it will actually, you will overfit the data. Because it will keep increasing K and then it will never end.

children.title: Evaluating Cluster Quality: Silo Head Score and Silhouette Score
children.summary: The segment discusses methods for determining the optimal value of K in clustering, specifically using the elbow method, which involves plotting inertia against various K values to identify the point of diminishing returns. Additionally, the silo head score is introduced as a way to assess the quality of clusters by evaluating how well a data point is clustered with its peers while maintaining separation from other clusters. The silhouette score, or silhouette width, is also explained as a measure of how closely a data point is associated with its cluster compared to other clusters, with higher scores indicating better clustering.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What you will do is you will use this album method on this screen plot in order to find the optimum value for K that you're observing diminishing return afterwards. There is another way for us to control K or identify K. That's the score that we can assign and that's called the silo head score. What is the silo head score? Silo head score says that I'm interested in just evaluating quality of clusters. I'm going to evaluate quality of the clusters for every data point that is clustered in this entire setting. So what we would say is that we would say that if I'm assigning a score to this data point right here in the center, it is vell cluster with the data points in the same cluster. And it has enough separation from the data points in another cluster. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin

children.title: Silhouette Scores for Clustering Evaluation
children.summary: The silhouette score, or silhouette width, is used to evaluate how well a specific data point is clustered. A high silhouette score indicates that a data point is close to others in its own cluster, while a low score suggests it is far from its cluster members. The score is determined by two components: A, the average distance to other points in the same cluster, which should be small for cohesion, and B, the average distance to points in the nearest opposing cluster, which should be large for separation. This dual evaluation helps assess the quality of clustering for each data point.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we would say that the clustering for this data point is cohesive. So therefore, I'm expecting a high silloy this for. For another data point, for example, this data point right here, We would say that it's not very close to the data points in its own cluster, but it is close to the data points in the opposing cluster. So it has two bad things going on. So it's not cohesive within its own cluster. It doesn't see the separation from another cluster. So that's why it is going to have a low stilloydiscord. So the stilloydiscord is going to use two pieces of information. So remember that I was talking about two things. So you should be vell cluster within the data points of your own cluster and you should have enough separation from the data points in the opposing cluster or the nearest cluster. So I'm going to refer to them but a. and B A is referring to the average distance to other points in the same cluster. So A needs to actually be a small value. So this smaller is the better because your distance to your own, to the data points to your own cluster should be small, meaning that you're very cohesive. of the point to average distance to the points in the closest cluster. So in this case, I have two clusters only. So A is going to look at the own cluster and then B is going to look at all of the points in the opposing cluster. For another data point, for example, this data point right here, We would say that it's not very close to the data points in its own cluster, but it is close to the data points in the opposing cluster. So it has two bad things going on. So it's not cohesive within its own cluster. It doesn't see the separation from another cluster. So that's why it is going to have a low stilloydiscord. So the stilloydiscord is going to use two pieces of information. So remember that I was talking about two things. So you should be vell cluster within the data points of your own cluster and you should have enough separation from the data points in the opposing cluster or the nearest cluster. So I'm going to refer to them but a. and B A is referring to the average distance to other points in the same cluster. So A needs to actually be a small value. So this smaller is the better because your distance to your own, to the data points to your own cluster should be small, meaning that you're very cohesive. of the point to average distance to the points in the closest cluster. So in this case, I have two clusters only. So A is going to look at the own cluster and then B is going to look at all of the points in the opposing cluster.

children.title: Understanding Silhouette Scores in Clustering
children.summary: The silhouette score is a metric for evaluating clustering quality, defined as (B - A) / max(A, B), where A is the average distance to points in the same cluster and B is the average distance to the nearest cluster. A high silhouette score indicates effective clustering, while a low score suggests poor clustering. A negative score indicates A is greater than B, often due to overfitting and misclassification. The score ranges from -1 to 1, with the ideal scenario being a score of 1, where all points in a cluster are identical.

children.title: Understanding Silhouette Scores in Clustering
children.summary: The silhouette score is a metric for evaluating how well a data point is clustered, defined as (B - A) / max(A, B), where A is the average distance to points in the same cluster and B is the average distance to the nearest cluster. A high silhouette score indicates good clustering, while a low score suggests poor clustering. A negative silhouette score occurs when A is greater than B, often due to overfitting, leading to misclassification. The score ranges from -1 to 1, with the best scenario being a score of 1 when all points in a cluster are identical.

children.title: Understanding Silhouette Scores in Clustering
children.summary: The silhouette score is a metric used to evaluate how well a data point is clustered. It is defined as (B - A) divided by the maximum of A and B, where A is the average distance to other points in the same cluster and B is the average distance to points in the nearest cluster. A high silhouette score indicates that a point is close to other points in its cluster, while a low score suggests it is far from its cluster. The aim is to maximize the separation between clusters, ensuring a substantial distance between data points in different clusters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And what we want to actually have here is that we want to make sure that our separation from the data points in the other cluster be a larger value. we decrease A. So the silloy the score is defined as this. B minus a divided by maximum of A and B. score is going to be a large value, right? And then dividing it by maximum is that we want to make sure that the distance or the separation between the data points in A and B is substantial. So that's why we're looking at the maximum between A and B as well as the denominator. And then actually if B is a large value, so the larger the denominator is, this smaller, the entire silhouette of the score is going to be. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

children.title: Understanding Silhouette Scores in Clustering
children.summary: The silhouette score evaluates how well a specific data point is clustered by calculating the distance between points within the same cluster and those in the nearest neighboring cluster. The formula for the silhouette score is (B - A) / max(A, B), where A is the average distance to points in the same cluster and B is the average distance to points in the nearest cluster. The best-case scenario for a silhouette score occurs when all points in a cluster are identical, resulting in a score of 1. If B is much larger than A, the score approaches 1 as well, indicating strong clustering.

The best case scenario is that all of the data points in the same cluster are on top of each other. So your distance to the data points in your own cluster is equal to zero, right? If your the distance between you and the data points in the same cluster is equal to zero and I'm going to write it here, it was b minus a divided by maximum of a m b. children.title: Negative Silhouette Score Explanation
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the conditions under which a silhouette score can be negative. It explains that a negative silhouette score occurs when the average distance of a data point to its own cluster (A) is greater than the average distance to the nearest cluster (B). This situation can arise, particularly in cases of overfitting during training, leading to misclassification during testing. The worst-case scenario is when all data points in the opposing cluster are closely packed together, resulting in a silhouette score that can range down to negative one.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Can the stilloidless score be a negative value? Because we're trying to find a wrench. Think about this lowest scoring point. Can there be a case that this low scoring point? Have a negative silloy discord. answer is yes. And we can actually look at the cases that it can be negative. For example, in this case, the Silla discourse is negative point one tree because it's just that A in this case is greater than B. Because if you're looking at the average distance of this point to all of these other points, these distances seems to be larger than the distance of this point to other cluster that we have. So here we're measuring B, here we're measuring A, and A is a larger value than B, and therefore A minus B divided by maximum of AMB it just really doesn't matter what the denominator is the numerator is going to be less than zero the worst case scenario can happen when all of the data points in the opposing cluster are on top of each other right and you are also on top of that right and your misclustered so that's the worst case scenario it will happen very rarely it will happen it will probably happen in the cases that you train something on the training data you overfitted to the training data and at the test time your misclassifying a data point so in all of those cases We can actually have a negative silhouette score up to negative one. leaf_chunk.audio: Can the stilloidless score be a negative value? Because we're trying to find a wrench. Think about this lowest scoring point. Can there be a case that this low scoring point? Have a negative silloy discord. answer is yes. And we can actually look at the cases that it can be negative. For example, in this case, the Silla discourse is negative point one tree because it's just that A in this case is greater than B. Because if you're looking at the average distance of this point to all of these other points, these distances seems to be larger than the distance of this point to other cluster that we have. So here we're measuring B, here we're measuring A, and A is a larger value than B, and therefore A minus B divided by maximum of AMB it just really doesn't matter what the denominator is the numerator is going to be less than zero the worst case scenario can happen when all of the data points in the opposing cluster are on top of each other right and you are also on top of that right and your misclustered so that's the worst case scenario it will happen very rarely it will happen it will probably happen in the cases that you train something on the training data you overfitted to the training data and at the test time your misclassifying a data point so in all of those cases We can actually have a negative silhouette score up to negative one.

children.title: Silhouette Score vs. Inertia in Clustering
children.summary: The silhouette score ranges from -1 to 1 and helps evaluate clustering quality by comparing the average distance of a point to its own cluster with the average distance to the nearest cluster. A negative silhouette score indicates that a point is closer to a different cluster than its own. In contrast, inertia measures the sum of squared distances from data points to their cluster center, decreasing as the number of clusters (k) increases, which can lead to overfitting and does not account for cluster separation. The elbow method is suggested for determining the optimal number of clusters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the range for the silhouette score as we saw is values between negative one and one. But how can we actually use the siloid score? And how is siloid score different than the inertia that we had? Remember inertia was not giving us what optimum K we can use. It was actually overfitting. It was actually using K in order to overfit to our data. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterini

children.title: Evaluating Clustering: Inertia and Silhouette Score
children.summary: Inertia measures the sum of squared distances from each data point to its cluster center, with lower values indicating tighter clusters. It decreases as the number of clusters (k) increases, which does not penalize overfitting. However, inertia does not account for the separation or shape of clusters. The elbow method is suggested for determining the optimal number of clusters. The Silhouette Score assesses how well a data point fits within its own cluster compared to neighboring clusters, with higher scores indicating better clustering quality.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Because as K was increasing, the inertia was decreasing. So we did not use inertia in order to find K, but what we did was we used elbow method in order to find out what is the optimum value is going to be in what inertia value that's gonna give us. With the siloid score, the higher the siloid score on average for all of the data points, that means that our data points are clustered well together. So it's given and they're blobby, they're separated and so on and so forth. So we consider the cohesiveness of the clusters and we also consider the separation. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

children.title: Evaluating Clustering with Silhouette Score
children.summary: To evaluate clustering results, we can calculate the silhouette score for different values of k. The silhouette score measures how well a data point fits within its cluster compared to neighboring clusters, with higher scores indicating better-defined clusters. It is useful for comparing clustering results and can highlight misclassified points with negative scores. Additionally, inertia is introduced as a loss function, representing the sum of squared distances from data points to their cluster centers, which decreases as k increases but does not account for cluster separation. The elbow method can also be used to determine the optimal number of clusters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it's kind of guaranteed. So one thing that we can do is if we are on this side of between two different k values, and they're equally good. So for example, in the data set, we'd seen that for k equal to 2k equal to 3, we were unsure which one of them we should pick. One thing that we can do is we can actually calculate the silhouette of score for each one of those k values and then figure out in each one of those scenarios how well the average silhouette of score is than therefore decide about what is the right value of kick. So speaking of deciding between the siloed between k equal to 2 and k equal to 3, this plot right here is a siloed plot. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

leaf_chunk.audio: So it's kind of guaranteed. So one thing that we can do is if we are on this side of between two different k values, and they're equally good. So for example, in the data set, we'd seen that for k equal to 2k equal to 3, we were unsure which one of them we should pick. One thing that we can do is we can actually calculate the silhouette of score for each one of those k values and then figure out in each one of those scenarios how well the average silhouette of score is than therefore decide about what is the right value of kick.
