O
Evaluating Clustering
abo
T
To evaluate different clustering results, we need a loss function.
2415657
We discussed:
·
Inertia: Sum of squared distances from each data point to its center.
O
Lower inertia indicates tighter clusters.
O
Inertia always decreases as k increases, so it doesn't penalize overfitting.
Inertia doesn't consider whether clusters are well-separated or non-spherical.
O
Use the elbow method to find optimal number of clusters.
Silhouette Score: Silhouette score evaluates how well a point fits within its cluster versus
how poorly it fits into neighboring clusters.
O
A higher silhouette score indicates better-defined and separated clusters.
0
Useful when comparing clustering results for different values of k.
O
Negative silhouette scores can highlight misclassified points or overlapping clusters.
Assumes convex clusters and computationally expensive.

children.title: Interpreting Silhouette Plots in Clustering
children.summary: The segment discusses the interpretation of silhouette plots in clustering analysis, highlighting that students are not required to draw these plots but should understand their meaning. It explains that the silhouette scores indicate how well data points are clustered, with larger silhouette widths suggesting that points are well-embedded within their respective clusters. The average silhouette score is represented by a red dotted line, and the segment references specific clusters, including orange and blue, to illustrate the variation in silhouette coefficient values across different data points.

children.title: Understanding Silhouette Plots in Clustering
children.summary: The segment discusses the interpretation of silhouette plots for clustering analysis. It explains that the arcs in the plot correspond to different clusters, with the y-axis representing cluster labels and the number of data points in each cluster. A larger number of data points in the blue cluster compared to the orange cluster is noted. Additionally, the silhouette scores for data points are highlighted, indicating that points with larger silhouette widths are well-embedded in their respective clusters. The red dotted line represents the average silhouette score across clusters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what you're seeing is that one. Part of it, this arc refers to the blue cluster and one arc refers to the orange cluster. On the y-axis you're seeing that the cluster labels, clusters, you run one, whatever you label before, and then you're also seeing that these numbers refer to how many data points you have in that cluster. So the y-axis, this cluster, or this arc is, that means that more data points are clustered into blue cluster as opposed to this orange cluster. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

children.title: Silhouette Scores and Clustering Analysis
children.summary: The segment discusses the calculation of silhouette scores for data points within clusters, indicating that there are approximately 100 data points in the blue cluster and 50 in the orange cluster. It explains that the arcs represent the silhouette scores for each data point, highlighting that a specific data point has a silhouette score of negative 0.1. The silhouette plot is introduced, showing that points with large silhouette widths are well-embedded in their clusters, with the red dotted line representing the average score. The plot illustrates the silhouette coefficient values across different clusters, specifically noting the distribution of scores for both the orange and blue clusters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And you can actually calculate, it's roughly between 10 and 110, so we're roughly having 100 data points in the blue cluster and then we're roughly having 50 data points in the orange cluster. Then how did we come up with the arcs? The arcs are actually sorted, siloediskores for all of the data points in that specific cluster. Remember that I was saying that this has a siloediskore of negative 0.1 tree. That's where that is, negative 0.1 tree. That's the indication of that data point. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

children.title: Silhouette Scores and Cluster Analysis
children.summary: Silhouette scores are used to evaluate the clustering of data points, indicating how well each point is embedded in its cluster. The discussion highlights that data points within the blue cluster are closely grouped, while the orange cluster shows no negative silhouette scores, suggesting all points are well-clustered. A silhouette plot can be created to visualize these scores, with higher scores indicating stronger cluster membership. The plot also includes an average score represented by a red dotted line, providing a reference for assessing the clustering quality.

So that's what's happening on average with the blue cluster. With the orange cluster, there are veil cluster with each other. children.title: Silhouette Scores and Cluster Quality Comparison
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the silhouette scores of data points within two clusters, emphasizing the importance of separation between them. It is noted that the orange cluster exhibits better quality than the blue cluster, as indicated by the silhouette plot. The plot illustrates that points with larger silhouette widths are well embedded in their respective clusters, with the red dotted line representing the average silhouette score for the clusters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It is veil separated from the other cluster as well. So the silhouette of scores are larger. So we're seeing that all of these data points are all of these silhouette of scores also are plotted on this kind of curve on the orange plot or on the orange cluster. What we want to actually see is we want to actually see the separation between these two clusters. We want to actually look at the average kind of siloid discourse for all of the data points in one cluster versus this other cluster. children.title: Silhouette Scores and Cluster Evaluation
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the evaluation of clusters using silhouette scores, emphasizing the importance of achieving a high average silhouette score across clusters. It is noted that the blue cluster scores significantly below the average, while the orange cluster scores well above it, indicating an imbalance. The silhouette plot illustrates these scores, where points with large silhouette widths are well embedded in their respective clusters, and the red dotted line represents the average silhouette score.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We're also observing that there is a bunch of different data points with negative siloid discourse, which is not ideal. ideal. So if you want to consider that or take that into consideration when you're deciding about K, all of those are good indications. Then this dotted line right here or dashed line is the average silhouette of all of the points between all of those clusters. So we want to make sure that this average silhouette, a score is as high as possible. And we also want to make sure that all of the or most of the clusters are contributing to that average. What you're seeing right here is that The blue cluster is way above the average. And then the, sorry, the orange cluster is way above the average. And then the blue cluster is way below the average. We would have liked to see more balance between these two clusters. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

children.title: Analyzing Silhouette Scores for Clustering
children.summary: The discussion focuses on evaluating the silhouette scores for three clusters, highlighting the performance of the blue, orange, and green clusters. It notes that the blue cluster has some negative scores due to a data point on the border, while the orange cluster's scores remain consistent, with slight adjustments in proximity to the closest cluster. The average silhouette score for all points is lower than before, indicating a potential issue when determining the optimal number of clusters using methods like the elbow method or dendrogram analysis.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But what happens if I go ahead and do three clusters? So this is three cluster. And then you're seeing the cluster or the siloet plot for the siloet scores of those three clusters. So in this case, you're seeing that there is this blue cluster. It looks like that it is still has some negative score, probably something to deal with. This data point right here on the border, and then you're seeing all of these silhouette values for blue, for orange, and then for green cluster. So you're seeing that the orange cluster remains roughly the same compared to this one, orange cluster stays the same. It's just that the closest cluster now is a little bit closer than before. So the average the scores are going to be shrinking slightly. And then between the green cluster and the blue cluster and combining them to what it was before with the blue cluster, we're still seeing that the performance is not comparable. But then if you look at the average silhouette score for all of the points, the average silhouette score is less than what it was before. So that is the case that it will tell you that, OK, when you're looking at the elbow method, or when you're looking at the dendrogram, And you are sure whether you pick K equal to 2 or K equal to 3. leaf_chunk.audio: But what happens if I go ahead and do three clusters? So this is three cluster. And then you're seeing the cluster or the siloet plot for the siloet scores of those three clusters. So in this case, you're seeing that there is this blue cluster. It looks like that it is still has some negative score, probably something to deal with. This data point right here on the border, and then you're seeing all of these silhouette values for blue, for orange, and then for green cluster. So you're seeing that the orange cluster remains roughly the same compared to this one, orange cluster stays the same. It's just that the closest cluster now is a little bit closer than before. So the average the scores are going to be shrinking slightly. And then between the green cluster and the blue cluster and combining them to what it was before with the blue cluster, we're still seeing that the performance is not comparable. But then if you look at the average silhouette score for all of the points, the average silhouette score is less than what it was before. So that is the case that it will tell you that, OK, when you're looking at the elbow method, or when you're looking at the dendrogram, And you are sure whether you pick K equal to 2 or K equal to 3.

children.title: Evaluating Clustering Techniques and T-Shirt Size Decisions
children.summary: The discussion evaluates silhouette scores for clustering, comparing K = 2 and K = 3, highlighting the subjective nature of cluster configuration choices and suggesting the average silhouette score while examining individual clusters. It also addresses decision making in T-shirt size selection, balancing design costs and profits between three and five size options. Clustering is introduced as a machine learning goal with two approaches: Cayman’s clustering, which is fast but may not achieve a global optimum, and K-Means. An example from T-shirt production analyzes clustering with K=3 (Small, Medium, Large) and K=5 (XS, S, M, L, XL), emphasizing cost and sales projections for optimal cluster determination. K-Means and Hierarchical Agglomerative clustering are explained, focusing on K-Means' optimization of inertia and the need for hyperparameter K in hierarchical methods. Upcoming topics on neural networks and language models are announced, along with Thanksgiving wishes for students.

children.title: Evaluating Silhouette Scores for Clustering
children.summary: The discussion focuses on evaluating silhouette scores for clustering, particularly comparing K = 2 and K = 3. It highlights the subjective nature of comfort in choosing between different cluster configurations and suggests favoring the average silhouette score while also emphasizing the importance of examining individual clusters.

children.title: Choosing Silhouette Scores for Clustering
children.summary: The discussion focuses on evaluating silhouette scores for clustering, specifically comparing K = 2 and K = 3. It emphasizes the subjective nature of comfort in choosing between different cluster configurations. The speaker suggests favoring the average silhouette score while acknowledging the importance of examining individual clusters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This is a way to go. Just look at the shape of the silhouette, silhouette the score plot, and then you see which one you're more comfortable with. And again, being comfortable what is really subjective. So me deciding between these two, probably I would just go with the average silhouette the score and just not look at what's happening within each one of those individual clusters. children.title: Decision Making in T-Shirt Size Selection and Clustering Techniques
children.summary: The segment discusses decision making in T-shirt size selection, emphasizing the balance between design costs and potential profits when comparing three versus five size options. It introduces clustering as a machine learning goal, detailing two approaches: Cayman's clustering, which is fast but may not achieve a global optimum, and K-Means. An example from T-shirt production illustrates the analysis of clustering with K=3 (Small, Medium, Large) and K=5 (XS, S, M, L, XL), stressing the importance of cost and sales projections in determining the optimal number of clusters. The lecture also covers K-Means and Hierarchical Agglomerative clustering, explaining K-Means' focus on optimizing inertia and the need for a hyperparameter K in hierarchical methods, with practical examples to aid selection. Lastly, it announces upcoming topics on neural networks and language models, wishing students a happy Thanksgiving.

children.title: Decision Making in T-Shirt Size Selection
children.summary: When deciding on t-shirt sizes for a company, it's important to consider both the cost of designing different size options and the potential profit from each. A comparison between offering three sizes versus five sizes should be made, taking into account the data and the specific application context. Ultimately, the decision may not solely rely on data analysis, as practical considerations and the intended use case play a significant role.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So I want to end on this note that you can always consider the context as well. So if you have a company and you want to decide key shirt sizes and you want to decide whether you should do three size t-shirts, a small medium large or you do five t-shirt sizes. Obviously what they would do is they would actually calculate the cost of designing five versus three and then calculating the profit and then which the one that gives more profit is going to be the one that you're choosing. So not everything at the end of the day is going to be about the data. There is to some extent that you can propose that, okay, probably three and four are the better options. And four is not three and five are better options. And four to share sizes is not going to be ideal. But then making a decision about the final kind of use case is going to just depend on the application that you're using it for. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

leaf_chunk.audio: So I want to end on this note that you can always consider the context as well. So if you have a company and you want to decide key shirt sizes and you want to decide whether you should do three size t-shirts, a small medium large or you do five t-shirt sizes. Obviously what they would do is they would actually calculate the cost of designing five versus three and then calculating the profit and then which the one that gives more profit is going to be the one that you're choosing. So not everything at the end of the day is going to be about the data. There is to some extent that you can propose that, okay, probably three and four are the better options. And four is not three and five are better options. And four to share sizes is not going to be ideal. But then making a decision about the final kind of use case is going to just depend on the application that you're using it for.

children.title: Clustering Algorithms and Choosing K
children.summary: The lecture covered clustering as a new machine learning goal, discussing two solutions: Cayman's clustering, which is fast but may not yield a global optimum, and K-Means. An example was provided involving a T-shirt production line where different clustering approaches (K=3 for Small, Medium, Large and K=5 for XS, S, M, L, XL) were analyzed. The decision on the number of clusters (K) should be guided by projected costs and sales to maximize profit.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's it today with the SQS clustering. We saw one full algorithm. We had seen two solutions. We had seen Cayman's clustering, very useful iterative approach, very fast to run compared to a collaborative clustering that we had seen next. We know that it has the issue that it might not give us the global optimum that we want. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X

children.title: Clustering Techniques: K-Means and Hierarchical Agglomerative
children.summary: The discussion focused on clustering as a machine learning goal, highlighting two primary solutions: K-Means and Hierarchical Agglomerative clustering. K-Means aims to optimize a loss function known as inertia, though no efficient algorithm exists to determine the optimal solution. The hierarchical approach was also covered, emphasizing the need for a hyperparameter K, which can be intuitively selected using examples like U.S. states, real-world metrics, and specific use cases.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We had seen hierarchical approach. We had seen different linkage criteria and we had also seen that it is useful in the cases that came in this clustering is going to fail. We also talked about how we picked the parameter K, hyperparameter K. We said that we can actually intuitively decide in the example of the states in the United States. We can use the Albumet that's in the Loid score. We can also use real-world metrics and profit and the use case that we're dealing with. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin X
+

children.title: Upcoming Topics: Neural Networks and Language Models
children.summary: The next session will cover neural networks, connecting concepts from logistic regression and combining multiple logistic functions. Demos of neural networks will be presented, followed by a discussion on large language models in the subsequent class. The instructor wishes everyone a happy Thanksgiving and encourages students to enjoy their break.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That's it for today. I'm going to come back on Tuesday after Thanksgiving and we're going to talk about neural networks. We're going to actually connect what you'd seen in logistic regression and then combine multiple logistic functions in order to build a neural network and we'll see fun and cool demos of neural network and then once you learn that you will easily learn large language models that we have on Thursday. Thank you for coming. Happy Thanksgiving. Enjoy the break. Video: 1: Lec 26 - DS100 Fa24 - Clusterin
